Preface. 


rpHE  four  years  which  have  elapsed  since  the  publication,  of  the  first  volume 
of  the  Bibliotheca  Cornubiensis  have  been  spent  by  ita^  authors  in  passing 
through  the  press  the  pages  of  its  successor.  Mindful  of  the  favourable  recep- 
tion accorded  to  the  first  volume  of  their  work,  the  Authors  have  adhered  almost 
without  variation  to  the  principles  which  guided  them  in  its  compilation.  They 
have  ventured  however  to  incorporate  in  this  volume  a  large  quantity  of  biogra- 
phical information  concerning  the  eminent  Cornishmen  mentioned  in  its  pages. 
They  found  that  the  enquiries  which  they  prosecuted  without  cessation  in  every 
part  of  the  county  of  Cornwall  for  information  as  to  the  lives  and  publications  of 
local  authors,  placed  them  in  possession  of  many  facts  which  would  undoubtedly 
have  been  forgotten  in  a  few  years.  It  was  their  first  intention  to  have  post- 
poned the  publication  of  this  additional  information,  but  they  gradually  arrived  at 
the  conclusion,  that  to  print  it  in  the  present  work  would  ensure  its  safe  preser- 
vation for  future  generations,  and  make  their  labours  not  less  acceptable  to  the 
generality  of  their  readers. 

The  biographical  details  and  lists  of  works  now  presented  to  public  criticism, 
have  been  corrected,  in  the  case  of  living  writers,  by  the  authors  themselves ; 
when  through  death  or  absence  from  England  the  benefit  of  this  personal  super- 
vision could  not  be  obtained,  the  lists  have  been  submitted  to  the  correction  of 
their  friends  and  relations.  If  this  circumstance  has  in  some  degree  delayed  the 
appearance  of  this  work  it  has  unquestionably  increased  the  accuracy  of  its  state- 
ments.    All  the  biographical  incidents  of  importance  mentioned  in  the  numerous 


IV 


histories  of  Cornwall  have  been  embodied  in  these  pages,  and  great  assistance  has 
been  obtained  from  the  family  papers  which  have  been  submitted  to  the  inspec- 
tion of  the  authors,  as  well  as  from  the  parish  registers  which  the  clergymen  of 
the  county  have  generously  permitted  them  to  consult.  The  reader  will  easily 
recognise  the  valuable  information  which  has  been  derived  from  the  matriculation 
registers  of  the  University  of  Oxford  concerning  the  Cornish  students  educated 
at  that  university.  For  the  possession  of  these  valuable  data,  the  importance  of 
which  it  would  be  difficult  to  overestimate,  the  authors  gratefully  acknowledge 
that  they  are  indebted  to  the  courtesy  of  Col.  Chester,  LL.D.  At  the  sister 
university  the  greater  part  of  the  Cornish  undergraduates  entered  their  names  on 
the  books  of  Pembroke  College;  the  facts  contained  in  these  entries  have  been 
kindly  supplied  by  the  present  Master  of  that  college. 

The  list"'  of  some  of  the  eminent  Cornishmen  comprised  in  this  volume  will 
be  found  to  illustrate  every  epoch  of  English  history,  and  every  branch  of  English 
thought.  A  comparison  of  their  names  with  those  printed  in  the  preface  to  the 
preceding  volume  will  shew  that  there  is  no  diminution  of  interest  in  these  pages. 
Although  it  is  unnecessary  to  indicate  in  detail  the  excellencies  of  these  dis- 
tinguished Cornishmen,  it  may  be  well  to  point  out  that  to  the  enquirer  into  the 
connection  with  England  of  the  early  settlers  in  America,  two  names,  Hugh  Peters 
and  Samuel  Penhallow,  are  of  especial  value.  The  last  labour  of  the  literary  life 
of  Isaac  DTsraeli — a  labour  which  has  unfortunately  not  yet  been  published — was

an  attempt  to  vindicate  the  character  of  Hugh  Peters  from  the  aspersions  cast 
upon  his  fame  by  the  pamphleteers  of  the  Eestoration.  In  the  absence  of  any 
adequate  attempt  to  do  justice  to  the  memory  of  this  remarkable  man  the  authors 
have  endeavoured,  and  they  hope  not  without  success,  to  collect  the  chief  refer- 
ences to  his  busy  life  which  can  be  found  in  the  literature  of  England  and 
America.  The  biographical  incidents  in  his  varied  career  are  followed  by  precise 
references  to  ninety-four  separate  works  describing  his  thoughts  and  his  acts 
while  the  bibliography  of  his  writings  in  print  and  manuscript  fills  fourteen 
columns  of  this  volume.  The  memoir  of  Samuel  Penhallow  (a  member  of  a 
family  entered  in  the  Herald's  Visitation  of  1620,  and,  like  the  unfortunate 
Hugh  Peters,  related  to  the  most  distinguished  families  of  Cornwall),  is  worthy 
of  notice  as  describing  a  Cornish  emigrant  who  rose  to  the  highest  post  afforded 
by  the  profession  of  the  law  in  New  England,  and  published  one  of  the  scarcest 
books  printed  in  America.  In  this  volume  wiU  also  be  found  the  bibliography 
of  a  few  eminent  authors  whose  connection  with  Cornwall  was  due  to  residence 
rather  than  to  birth.  The  name  of  Eobert  Paltock,  still  only  nominis  umbra,  was 
disclosed  in  1835  as  the  author  of  a  romantic  narrative,  often  reprinted  in  the 
last  century  for  the  charm  which  it  threw  round  the  adventures  of  a  pseudo- 
native  of  Cornwall.  To  the  seclusion  of  a  Cornish  vicarage  on  the  river  Fal  the 
Eev.  W.  J.  Temple  retired  from  intimacy  with  Johnson  and  Boswell,  and  from 
another  remote  parsonage  on  the  same  river  John  Whitaker  issued  those  antiqua- 
rian and  biographical  volumes  which  provoked  the  censure  or  admiration  of  the 
book-lovers  of  his  time.  With  the  city  of  Truro  the  fame  of  the  unwearied 
evangelist,  Samuel  Walker,  is  inseparably  associated.  If  Cornwall  recognizes  with 
legitimate  pride  that  the  list  of  her  illustrious  sons  includes  the  name  of  Dr. 
S.  P.  TregeUes,  she  also  claims  to  have  nurtured  for  many  years  the  labours  in 
biblical  criticism  of  the  Eev.  F.  H.  A.  Scrivener.  To  the  writings  of  these  dis- 
tinguished residents  in  Cornwall  the  authors  have  devoted  especial  attention ;  they 
add  with  pleasure  that  the  list  of  Whitaker's  publications  was  submitted  to  the 
critical  examination  of  Mr.  James  Crossley,  F.S.A.  and  Mr.  J.  E.  Bailey,  F.S.A., 
of  Manchester. 

Whilst  the  authors  have  been  engaged  in  the  protracted  task  of  super- 


VI 


intending  the  printing  of  these  pages,  they  have  been  deprived  of  the  assistance 
of  many  gentlemen  who  aided  them  in  the  compilation  of  the  first  volume,  and 
encouraged  them  with  their  approbation.  They  cannot  refrain  from  placing  on 
record  the  irreparable  loss  which  they  sustained  by  the  deaths  of  Mr.  Charles 
Chorley,  Mr.  W.  J.  Henwood,  F.E.S.,  and  Mr.  William  Sandys,  F.S.A.  The 
rest  of  the  friends  and  correspondents  particularized  in  the  preface  to  the  first 
volume  have  continued  to  respond  to  the  demands  which  must  often  have 
trespassed  in  no  inconsiderable  degree  on  their  valuable  time ;  and  as  the 
publication  of  the  first  volume  proved  that  the  authors  were  sparing  neither 
time  nor  money  for  the  attainment  of  their  desire  to  do  justice  to  all  sections 
of  literary  life  in  Cornwall,  it  raised  up  for  them  willing  workers  in  nearly  every 
district  of  the  county.  To  specify  the  whole  of  their  coadjutors  would  be 
impossible,  but  they  must  not  omit  to  acknowledge  the  ready  assistance  which 
they  have  ever  obtained  from  the  local  knowledge  of  Eev.  W.  lago,  of  Bodmin, 
Mr.  M.  P.  Moyle,  and  Mr.  James  C  Cunnack,  of  Helston,  Mr.  Stephen  Clogg,  of 
East  Looe,  Mr.  Edward  Heard,  of  Truro,  Mr.  P.  A.  Renfree,  of  Redruth,  and  Mr. 
Thomas  Kinsman,  of  St.  Austell.  To  the  clergy  of  the  county  the  authors  beg 
to  renew  their  thanks  for  the  biographical  information  which  they  have  supplied 
from  the  pages  of  their  parish  registers.  The  works  now  described  have  for  the 
most  part  been  consulted  in  the  library  of  the  British  Museum,  but  the  treasures 
of  the  Bodleian  and  Dr.  Williams'  Library  have  not  unfrequently  remedied  the 
deficiencies  which  can  still  be  discovered  in  that  unrivalled  collection.  In  con- 
nection with  this  branch  of  their  enquiries  the  authors  must  not  fail  to  make 
grateful  mention  of  the  aid  which  they  have  received  from  Mr.  W.  H.  Allnutt, 
of  the  Bodleian  and  Mr.  Frederic  Boase,  of  the  Incorporated  Law  Society 
Library. 

The  preparation  of  the  third  and  concluding  volume  of  the  Bibliotheca 
Cornubiensis  has  far  advanced,  and  will  be  continued  with  as  much  speed  as 
is  consistent  with  a  due  regard  to  accuracy.  It  will  contain  an  alphabetical 
arrangement  of  miscellaneous  matter  which  could  not  be  classified  under  the 
names  of  the  authors,  a  list  of  local  acts  of  parliament  and  civil  war  pamphlets 
relating  to  the  county,  a  supplement  showing  the  works  of  recent  date  and  the 


vu 

particulars  which  have  been  brought  to  light  since  the  commencement  of  the 
printing  of  the  previous  volumes,  and  lastly,  a  complete  index  to  the  contents 
of  the  entire  work. 

The  authors  would  venture,  in  conclusion,  to  express  their  hope  that  any 
readers  discovering  errors  or  omissions  in  these  pages  will  kindly  communicate 
with  them. 


15,  Queen  Anne's  Gate,  Westminster,  8.W., 

January,  1878.

PAGET,  Rev.  Eusebius.  V.  of  Kilkhampton, 
deprived  1584;  E.  of  St.  Anne  and  St.  Agnes, 
Aldersgate,  London  1604-17.  b.  Cranford, 
Northamptonshire  circa  1542.  d.  London

The  life  and  adventures  of  Peter  Wilkins,  a 
Cornish  Man ;  relating  particularly,  his  ship- 
wreck near  the  South  Pole,  his  wonderful  passage 
thro'  a  subterraneous  cavern  into  a  kind  of  new 
world,  his  there  meeting  with  a  gawry  or  flying 
woman,  whose  life  he  preserv'd  and  afterwards 
married  her,  his  extraordinary  conveyance  to 
the  country  of  glums  and  gawrys,  or  men  and 
women  that  fly.  Likewise  a  description  of  this 
strange  country,  with  the  laws,  customs  and 
manners  of  its  inhabitants,  and  the  author's 
remarkable  transactions  among  them.  Taken 
from  his  own  mouth,  in  his  passage  to  England 
from  off  Cape  Horn  in  America  in  the  ship 
Hector.  With  an  introduction  giving  an  account 
of  the  surprizing  manner  of  his  coming  on  board 
that  vessel  and  his  death  on  his  landing  at 
Plymouth  in  the  year  1739.  Illustrated  with 
several  cuts,  clearly  and  distinctly  representing 
the  structure  and  mechanism  of  the  wings  of 
the  glums  and  gawrys,  and  the  manner  in  which 
they  use  them  either  to  swim  or  to  fly.  By  R.  S., 
a  passenger  in  the  Hector.  Lond.  printed  for 
J.  Robinson  at  the  Golden  Lion  in  Ludgate- 
street  and  R.  Dodsley  at  Tully's  Head  in  Pall 
Mall  1751,  2  vols.  12o.  6/-. 

The  life  and  adventures  of  Peter  Wilkins. 
Dublin,  George  Faulkner  1751,  2  vols.  12°.

PALTOCK,  OB  PULTOCK,  Eobeet.    (Cm.). 

Conquest.    Produced  at  the  Grecian  Theatre, 
London  24  Dec.  1857.   Notprintedl 

Note. — We  have  ascertained  that  no  person  of  tha 
name  of  E.  Paltock  was  admitted  a  member  of  Clement's 
Inn,  he  may,  however,  have  practised  there  as  a  Solicitor 
without  having  been  a  member,  or  he  may  been  simply 
a  resident  in  the  Inn.  The  first  ed.  of  this  work  is  said 
in  Watt  to  be  dated  1750,  but  we  have  not  seen  any 
copy  of  this  date.  In  that  of  1751  the  dedication  to  The 
Eight  Honourable  Elizabeth,  Countess  of  Northumber- 
land, is  signed  E.  P.  as  is  also  the  Introduction,  pp. 
iii-xiii,  and  although  it  has  been  suggested  that  the 
letters  E.  S.  on  the  title  are  a  telonism  of  Peter  Witkina, 
it  seems  more  probable  to  suppose  that  they  are  simply 
a  misprint  for  E.  P.  In  the  copy  dated  1751  in  the  Br. 
Museum  the  Dedication  is  bound  up  in  the  second 
volume.  The  authorship  of  this  work  was  unknown 
until  the  year  1835,  when  in  cataloguing  a  number  of 
books  and  manuscripts  which  formerly  belonged  to 
Dodsley  the  publisher,  Mr.  Nicol,  the  printer,  discovered 
the  original  agreement  for  the  sale  of  the  manuscript 
from  "Eobert  Paltock,  of  Clement's  Inn"  to  Jacob 
Eobinson  and  Dodsley.  The  price  given  for  the  entire 
copyright  was  £21,  twelve  copies  of  the  work  and  "cuts 
of  the  first  impression"  [i.e.  a  set  of  proofs  of  the 
engravings  in  the  first  edition].  The  assignment  in 
1854  was  penes  Mr.  James  Crossley  of  Manchester.

Prospectus. — A  Descriptive  Catalogue  of  the 
geological  specimens  deposited  in  the  Museum 
of  the  Royal  Geological  Society  of  Cornwall, 
interspersed  with  observations  tending  to  show 
the  osconomical  applications  of  geology  to  the 
agricultural,  mining  and_  commercial  interests  of 
the  county  of  Cornwall.  By  J.  A.  Paris . . .  Vigurs, 
Printer,  Penzance,  8°.,  9/-. 

Note. — Announced  for  publication  in  1817  but 
never  issued.  The  foundation  of  the  E.G.S.C.  had  its 
origin  in  1814  in  a  conversation  between  Dr.  Paris,  Mr. 
Ashhurst  Majendie  and  Mr.  H.  Boase. 

On  the  accidents  which  occur  in  the  mines  of 
Cornwall  in  consequence  of  the  premature  ex- 
plosion of  gunpowder  in  blasting  rocks  and  on 
the  methods  to  be  adopted  for  preventing  it,  by 
the  introduction  of  a  safety  bar  and  an  instru- 
ment termed  the  shifting  cartridge,  to  which  is 
appended  a  schedule  of  the  names,  circumstances 
and  injuries  of  a  large  proportion  of  those, miners 
who  have  within  a  few  years  suffered  from  such 
explosions  and  are  in  consequence  blind  or 
totally  disabled... By  J.  A.  Paris,  M.D.,  Secre- 
tary to  the  Eoyal  Geological  Society  of  Cornwall, 
by  whose  desire  this  essay  has  been  published. 
[Plate].  Penzance,  printed  and  sold  by  T.  Vigurs; 
sold  also  by  Baldwin,  Cradock  and  Joy,  London. 
...n.d.  [1817]  80, 

Note.— Title  pp.  1-2,  dedication  to  Sir  Rose  Price, 
Bart.  pp.  3-4,  on  the  accidents,  pp.  5-30,  a  schedule  of 
the  names  of  miners  who  have  suffered  from  accidental 
explosions  (principally  in  the  hundred  of  Penwith),  pp. 
1-14.

Note. — J.  Pearee,  John  Badcock  and  John  Davy

The  History  of  the  long  captivity  and  ad- 
ventures of  Thomas  Pellow  in  South-Barbary, 
giving  an  account  of  his  being  taken  by  two 
SaUee  Rovers  and  carry' d  a  slave  to  Mequinez 
at  eleven  years  of  age,  his  various  adventures 
in  that  country  for  the  space  of  twenty-three 
years ;  escape  and  return  home.  In  which  is 
introduced  A  particular  account  of  the  manners 
and  customs  of  the  Moors... Written  by  himself. 
Printed  for  E.  Goadby  and  sold  by  W.  Owen, 
Bookseller  at  Temple  Bar,  London,  n.d.  [17391] 
sm.  80.,  pp.  388,  5/-.— 2nd  ed.  Printed  for  E. 
Goadby... m.d  [17401)  sm.  8".,  pp.  388.— Another 
ed.  Lond.  Baldwin  1775,  12".,  3/-. 

Note. — T.  Pellow  sailed  from  Fowey  to  Genoa  with 
a  cargo  of  pilchards  in  the  "  Francis,"  Capt.  John 
Pellow,  the  owner  being  Velentine  Eues  of  Penryn.  It 
was  on  the  return  voyage  whilst  off  Cape  Finisterre  that 
he  was  taken  prisoner  by  the  Sallee,  Rovers.

Note. — Title  1  leaf.  The  translator's  preface  6  leaves, 
The  History  of  John  a-Leyden  pp.  1-61,  The  Postscript 
of  the  late  tumult  at  Abingdon,  signed  W.  Hughes, 
Hinton,  Berks  Dec.  26,  1656,  pp.  63-110.  The  tumult 
at  Abingdon  was  upon  the  sepulture  of  J.  Pendarves, 
his  body  having  been  brought  there  from  London  in  a 
large  chest  by  water.  The  concourse  of  people  being 
very  large  Major  General  Bridges  with  8  troops  of  horse 
was  sent  to  disperse  the  meeting  when  resistance  being 
offered  several  arrests  were  made.

island.     'To  which  is  added  an  appendix  con- 
taining Minutes  of  three  trials  which  occurred

the  condition  of  slaves  in  general  in  our  West-

at  Padstow.  d.  Bodmin  4  June  564.  cf.  Acta

33,  35,  44,  157,  701 ;  T.  D.  Hardy's  Catalogue 
of  Materials  ( Rolls' Series)  i,  pt.  i,  pp.  117-18

178,  180  (1867);  A.  Butler's  Lives  of  the  Saints 
(1838)  i,  737 ;  Journ.  R.LG.  Apl.  1872  p.  105 
on  the  casket  of  his  relics. 

Note. — St.  Petrock  is  said  to  have  written  a  work 
entitled  "De  vita  solitaria."  The  churches  dedicated 
to  S.  Petrock  are,  in  Cornwall,  Bodmin,  (originally  called 
Petrockstow)  Padstow,  Petherick,  (S.  Petrock  minor) 
Trevalga  in  Trigg  minor.  In  Devonshire,  Anstey  West, 
Bamptou,  South  Brent,  Charles,  Clanaborough,  Halla- 
combe,  Lidford,  Newton  S.  Petrock,  S.  Petrock  and  Pet- 
rockstow, S.  Petrock  in  Exeter.  S.  Petrock  is  said  to 
have  been  the  son  of  Clement,  a  Cornish  prince.

Note. — The  Petition  presented  to  the  House  of 
Commons  by  Thomas  Slingsby  Duncombe,  M.P.  for 
Finsbury  2  May  1842,  for  the  National  Convention  and 
signed  by  3,315,752  persons  was  written  by  E.  K.  Philp. 
This  the  most  numerously  signed  document  on  record 
was  borne  into  the  House  on  the  shoulders  of  sixteen 
stone  masons,  workmen  at  that  time  employed  in  build- 
ing the  Houses  of  Parliament.  The  concluding  prayer : 
of  the  petition  was,  that  as  there  were  no  working  class  ■ 
representatives  in  the  House,  the  memorialists  might  be 
heard  at  the  Bar,  in  support  of  the  prayer  of  the  peti-' 
tion.  But,  by  a  vote  of  287  against  49  the  House  on  the 
3  May  refused  to  allow  the  Petitioners  advocates  to  be 
heard.  E.  K.  Philp  was  one  of. the  three  persons  nomi- 
nated to  represent  the  working  classes  on  this  occasion, 
had  the  prayer  been  granted,  c/.  T.  C.  Hansard's  Pari, 
Debates  Ixii,  1374-81,  Ixiii,  14-91,  424  (1842) ;  The  An- 
nual Register  1842,  Chronicle  pp.  77-78 ;  Gent.  Mag. 
xvii,  656,  (1842);  Will.  N.  Molesworth's  History  of  Eng-. 
land  (1872)  ii,  368-69 ;  Joseph  Irving's  Annals  of  our 
time  (1871)  p.  107.

PITT,  Thomas.     (Con.). 

equal  to  £20,400.  He  sold  it  to  the  King  of  France  in  1716 
for  £135,000  or  according  to  some  accounts  £200,000; 
it  was  reckoned  at  that  time  the  largest  diamond  in 
Europe  and  is  said  to  have  weighed  127  carats,  when 
polished  it  was  as  large  as  a  pullet's  egg.  cf.  also  Davies 
Gilbert's  Hist,  of  Cornwall  (1838)  i,  68-72  where  is  a 
woodcut  of  the  Pitt  diamond.

also  from  the  poop  of  the  Victory,  with  a  musket,  shot 
the  Frenchman,  who  killed  Lord  Nelson.

Defence  against  the  attacks  of  Mr.  Overton  and  his 
friends.   Letter  signed  R.  Polwhele,  xx,  488-42  (180S). 

It  will  be  observed  that  R.  Polwhele  on  several 
occasions  wrote  reviews  of  his  own  works.  The  above 
reviews  are  assigned  to  R.  Polwhele  on  the  authority  of 
a  copy  of  The  Anti-Jacobin  Rev.  now  in  the  Br.  Mu- 
seum, in  which  the  articles  are  marked  by  John  Gifford, 
the  editor.

Prideaux's  consent.

PEIDEAUX,  Eev.  Humphrey.    (Con.). 

An  award  of  king  Charles  the  first,  under  his 
broad-seal,  settling  two  shillings  of  the  pound, 
out  of  the  rents  of  the  houses  in  Norwich,  for 
the  maintenance  of  the  parochial  clergy  of  that 
city,  in  lieu  of  personal  tithes.  With  A.  Treatise 
vindicating  the  legality  and  justice  of  that 
award,  and  showing  that  personal  tithes  in  lieu 
of  which  the  said  payment  of  two  shillings  of 
the  pound  was  awarded,  are  still  due  by  the  law 
of  the  land,  although  they  have  been  unjustly 
substracted  ever  since  the  Statute  of  the  2d  and 
3d  of  King  Edward  the  Sixth,  which  took  away 
the  oath  whereby  they  are  to  be  proved,  and 
that  there  is  a  necessity  of  again  restoring  them 
or  settling  something  in  lieu  of  them,  for  the 
maintenance  of  ministers  in  the  cities  and  large 
towns  of  the  realm.  By  H.  Prideaux,  D.D. ... 
Lond.  printed  for  Eobert  Clavel,  at  the  Peacock 
in  St.  Paul's  church-yard  1707,  4°.  Title  1  leaf.

The  plan  of  a  work  entitled  Mineralogia  Cor- 
nubiensis  et  Leges  Stannarise ...  by  W.  Pryce  of 
Redruth,  Cornwall.  Falmouth,  printed  for  the 
author  and  sold  by  the  booksellers  in  town  and 
country,  n.d.  [1773  or  1774]  8".  pp.  40.  Bodl 
lAb.  Gough's  Collection. 

Note. — The  proposal  speaks  of  the  intended  publi- 
cation in  1778  and  was  written  after  the  death  of  Dr. 
Borlase  the  antiquary.  In  it  Dr.  Pryce  mentions  that 
about  22  years  previously  he  first  attended  the  business 
proceedings  at  Dolcoath  Mine  and  that  for  17  years  he 
had  seldom  failed  of  his  attendance.  Amongst  the  pei'- 
Bons  who  assisted  him  in  his  undertaking  are  mentioned 
Mr.  John  Edwards  of  Hayle  Copper  Works,  rev.  Mr. 
Pickering  of  Eedruth,  Francis  Bennallack,  Dr.  Borlase, 
the  historian  and  Dr.  Borlase  the  vice  warden,  (his 
kinsmen)  and  Philip  Webber  of  Falmouth,  the  latter  of 
whom  is  spoken  of  as  "  father  and  protector  of  my 
orphan  state  during  a  long  minority."  Amongst  the 
persons  by  whom  subscriptions  would  be  received  is 
E.  Pryce  of  Shrewsbury.

PEYCE,  William.   (Con.). 

Mineralogia  Cornubiensis,  a  treatise  on  min- 
erals, mines  and  mining,  containing  the  theory 
and  natural  history  of  strata,  fissures  and  lodes 
with  the  methods  of  discovering  and  working  of 
tin,  copper  and  lead  mines  and  of  cleansing  and 
metalizing  their  products  shewing  each  parti- 
cular process  for  dressing,  assaying  and  smelting 
of  ores,  to  which  is  added  an  explanation  of  the 
terms  and  idioms  of  miners,  by  W.  Pryce  of 
Eedruth  in  Cornwall.  Lond.  printed  and  sold 
for  the  author  by  James  Phillips,  George  yard, 
Lombard-street...  1778,  fol.  42/-.

7  leaves,  Introduction  pp.  i-xv,  A  general  treatise  pp.

PEYNNE,  William.  Barrister-at-law.  M.P. 
Newport  1640;  Imprisoned  by  order  of  Oliver 
Cromwell's  government  at  Pendennis  Castle 
1651-52.  b.  Swainswick,  near  Bath  1600.  d. 
Lincoln's  Inn  24  Oct.  1669.  cf.  Bliss'  Wood 
iii,  843-77. 

A  new  discovery  of  the  prelates  tyranny  in 
their  late  prosecutions  of  Mr.  William  Pryn,  an 
eminent  lawyer ;  Dr.  lohn  Bastwick,  a  learned 
physician  and  Mr.  Henry  Burton,  a  reverent 
divine... and  manner  of  their  removes  to  and 
close  imprisonments  in  the  Castles  of  Launces- 
ton,  Lancaster,  Carnarvon  and  Isles  of  Sylly, 
Guernsey  and  Jersey... Printed  at  London  for 
M.S.  1641,  sm.  4". 

Note. —  Title  1  leaf,  To  the  courteous  reader  2 
leaves,  A  new  discovery  pp.  7-48,  A  brief  relation  of 
trial  in  the  Starre-chamber  etc.  pp.  1-226,  Errata  1 
leaf. 

Pendennis  and  all  other  standing  forts  dis- 
mantled or  eight  military  aphori,sms,  demon- 
strating the  vselessness,  unprofitableness,  hurt- 
fulness  and  prodigall  expen.siveness  of  all  stand- 
ing English  forts  and  garrisons  to  the  people  of 
England,  their  inability  to  protect  them  from 
invasions,  depredations  of  enemies  or  pyrates  by 
sea  or  land ;  the  great  mischiefs,  pressures,  in- 
conveniences they  draw  upon  the  inhabitants, 
country  and  adjacent  places  in  times  of  open 
wars,  when  pretended  mo,st  usefull  and  the 
grand  over-sight,  mistake,  injury  in  continuing 
them  for  the  present  or  future  reall  defence  of 
the  peoples  lives,  liberties,  estates,  the  only  ends 
pretended  for  them.  Penned  by  William  Prynne 
of  Swainswick,  esquire  during  his  close  imprison- 
ment in  Pendennis  Castle  and  now  pubhshed 
for  the  common  benefit,  ease,  information  of  the 
whole  nation... Lond.  printed  for  the  author  and 
are  to  be  sold  by  Edward  Thomas  in  Green- 
arbour  [5  Dec]  1657,  sm.  4". 

Note.— Title  1  leaf,  To  the  reader  2  leaves,  Aphor- 
isms (paffination  irregular)  20  leaves. 

W.  Prynne  after  relating  how  he  had  several  dis- 
courses with  the  officers  and  soldiers  concerning  the 
uselessness  of  forts  says  "I  did  thereupon  in  my  dark 
Pendennis  cell  (where  I  had  few  books  and  lesse  light 
to  read)  in  September  1652  to  passe  away  idle  hours 
digest  the  substance  of  my  discourses  against  forts  and 
garrisons.,  which  I  sent  from  thence  to  a  since  deceased 
friend  in  London."

Note.— Title  1  leaf,  Stuartiana,  pp.  1-108,  Index  2 
leaves.  Each  story  has  a  difierently  coloured  border, 
the  designs  of  which  as  well  as  the  woodcuts  and  the 
press  marks  in  the  work  are  the  invention  of  Jonathan 
Eashleigh,  who  revised  the  MS.  and  printed  it  at  his 
private  press  3  Cumberland  terrace,  Eegent's  park,  Lon- 
don. The  impression  being  of  25  copies  only  for  pri- 
vate circulation. 

The  writer  of  Stuartiana  was  the  hon.  Sophia 
Juliana  Stuart,  dau.  of  Thos.  Penn  of  Stoke  Pogis. 
She  TO.  Apl.  1796  the  hon.  and  most  rev.  Will.  Stuart, 
Archbishop  of  Armagh  and  Primate  of  all  Ireland,  who 
d  Hill  street,  Berkeley  square,  London  6  May  1822  aged 
68  Mrs.  Stuart  was  the  grandmother  of  Mary  Pole 
Stuart  (dau.  of  Villiers  Stuart  of  Aldenham  abbey, 
Herts,)  who  was  5.  Aldenham  abbey  23  Sep.  1822.  m. 
Aldenham  1  Aug,  1843  Jonathan  Eashleigh,  and  d.  3 
Cumberland  terrace  25  Jan.  1852  aged  29. 

Some  extracts  from  the  MS.  of  this  work  are  given 
in  J.  Heneage  Jesse's  "Memoirs  of  Geo.  in"  (1867)  m, 
230-33.

RASHLEIGH,  Capt.  P.    {Con.). 

Note. — On  the  fourth  page  of  the  preface  is  the 
following  relation  : — 

"  Only  I  requested  him  [the  arch-pirate  Peter 
Easton  in  1611]  to  release  a  ship  that  he  had  taken 
upon  the  coast  of  Guinnie,  belonging  to  one  Captaine 
Rashly  of  Poy  in  Cornewall,  a,  man  whom  I  knew,  but 
only  by  report ;  which  he  accordingly  released.  Where- 
upon I  prouided  men,  victuals  and  a  fraught  for  the 
said  ship  and  so  sent  her  home  to  Dartmouth  in  Deuon, 
though  I  neuer  had  so  much  as  thanks  for  my  kindnesse 
therein."  cf.  also  "  Westward  Hoe  for  Avalon."  Edited 
by  T.  Whitburn.  Lond.  1870,  8".  where  on  p.  12  the  above 
extract  is  also  found.

EAVERTY,  Major  Henry  George  (son  of 
Peter  Baverty  or  O'Baverty,  Assistant  surgeon 
B.N.  i.  Co.  Tyrone,  d.  at  sea,  bur.  in  the  island 
of  Santa  Martha  1829).  Educated  at  Classical 
school,  Falmouth  and  Penare  academy,  Pen- 
zance;  Of  H.M.  3rd  regt.  of  Bombay  native 
infantry.  Cadet  of  1843,  Ensign  26  June  1843, 
Lieut.  21  Jan.  1846,  Passed  both  standards 
of  examination  prescribed  for  officers  in  civil 
employment  in  1854  and  1855  respectively. 
Captain  28  June  1858,  Major  28  June  1863 ; 
Served  in  India  1843-60,  including  the  Pan- 
jab  campaign  1848-49,  the  siege  of  Multan 
and  the  battle  of  Guzerat  and  the  expedition 
on  the  Pes'hawar  frontier  1849-50  ;  Retired 
from  active  service  1865.  b.  Falmouth  31 
May  1827.  bapt.  Penwerris  district  church. 
Now  (Dec.  1875)  resident  at  Ormonde,  Lost- 
withiel. 

A  grammar  of  the  Puk'hto,  Pus'hto,  or  lan- 
guage of  the  Afghans ;  in  which  the  rules  are 
illustrated  by  examples  from  the  best  writers, 
both  poetical  and  prose,  together  with  transla- 
tions from  the  articles  of  war  and  remarks  on 
the  language,  literature  and  descent  of  the  Af- 
ghan tribes,  by  lieut.  H.  G.  Raverty... Assistant

EAWSON,  Sir  William  (youngest  son  of  Hen. 


EAWSON,  SiK  WiLLiAM.    (Con.). 

Adams).  Took  the  name  of  Eawson  in  compli- 
ance with  the  will  of  the  widow  of  Col.  Eaw- 
son and  by  royal  licence  and  authority  9  Mch. 
1825;  Assistant  to  Mr.  Hill  surgeon  at  Barn- 
staple; Pupil  of  J.  Cunningham  Saunders; 
M.D.  Edinb.  1800:  Assistant  surgeon  to  the 
infirmary  for  diseases  of  the  eye  Charter- 
house square  1805  ;  Founder  of  and  Surgeon 
to  West  of  England  Infirmary  for  curing  dis- 
eases of  the  eye,  Exeter  1809-10;  Then  at 
Bath  ;  Took  up  his  residence  in  London  1810; 
M.E.C.S.  Lond.  1810;  Surgeon  and  oculist 
extraordinary  to  the  Prince  Eegent  and  the 
Dukes  of  Kent  and  Sussex ;  Surgeon  to  the 
Ophthalmic  Institution,  York  Hospital,  Chel- 
sea; Knighted  at  Carlton  house  11  May  1814; 
Eesided  at  Mount  Clair,  Eoehampton,  Surrey 
and-  at  26  Albemarle  street,  h.  Stanbury, 
Morwinstow  5  Dec.  1783.  No  entry  of  bapt, 
in  register,  d.  Upper  Gloucester  place,  Dorset 
square,  London  4  Feb.  1827.  bur.  St.  John's

BEAD,  Thomas.     (Con.). 

St.  Mary's,  Penzance  there  is  no  record  of  the  burial 
of  Thomas  Eead  in  1811  and  no  such  person  was  ever 
sexton  there.  The  reference  is  probably  to  Tobias  Eead, 
hur.  St.  Marys  10  May  1810,  aged  62  and  entered  in 
the  books  as  the  "worthy  clerk  of  the  chapel."  This 
person  was  also  town  crier  and  every  Sunday  morning 
at  half  past  ten  went  round  the  town  ringing  his  bell 
to  give  notice  of  the  commencement  of  church  service.

EENNELL,  Major  J.     (Cm.). 

for  Lady  Rodd,   by  J.   G.   and   F.  Eivington 
1832,  8».  63/-. 

Note. — Title  1  leaf,  "  To  the  King,"  signed  "  Jane 
Eodd,  Brighton  11th  Feb.  1832,"  pp.  iii-iv.  Advertise- 
ment, signed  "  Jane  Eodd,  Wimpole  street  Aug.  6th 
1832,"  pp.  v-vi.  Account  of  the  charts  and  contents 
pp.  vii-viii.  Investigation  pp.  1-300.  App.  Observations 
on  currents  pp.  301-359.  The  seven  charts  of  currents 
which  this  work  is  designed  to  illustrate  are  Dedicated 
"  To  his  most  gracious  majesty  William  the  Fourth 
These  Charts  of  the  prevalent  currents  in  The  Atlantic 
Ocean,  constructed  by  the  late  major  Eennell  are  with 
special  permission,  dedicated  by  the  author's  affection- 
ate daughter  and  his  majesty's  dutiful  subject  Jane

nem  de  S.  Yictore." 

Pits  followed  by  Parkinson  makes  Eichard  Eufus 
a  different  person  from  Eichard  of  Cornwall,  alleg- 
ing that  he  was  6.  at  Cirencester  and  died  circa  1270, 
but  Leland  is  perhaps  right  in  identifying  them,  as  in 
Monumenta  Franciscana,  p.  550,  Eicardus  Eufus  Cor- 
nubiensis  is  expressly  named  and  the  same  acts  and 
writings  are  attributed  to  both.  It  appears  that  he 
could  not  speak  English  well,  as  he  mostly  spoke  Cor- 
nish.

Note. — Sarah  Robartes  brought  her  husband  at  her 
marriage  an  estate  worth  £30,000  and  he  died  without 
making  any  settlement  or  jointure  on  her.  She  brought 
a  writ  of  dower  to  recover  her  thirds  of  the  sixth  part 
of  divers  manors,  to  which  Abraham  Vandebendy,  the 
tenant,  pleaded  in  bar  a  term  of  99  years.  She  re- 
covered her  dower  at  law  against  this  land  but  again 
lost  it  in  chancery,  and  on  an  appeal  to  the  house  of 
lords  the  decree  of  dismission  was  affirmed,  cf.  also 
Journals  of  the  house  of  Lords  1707,  xviii,  340,  349-50, 
353,  y55.

Note. — Eobert  gave  the  Monastery  of  St.  Michael's 
Mount  in  Cornwall  to  the  monks  of  St.  Michael's  Mount 
in  Normandy  (St.  Michael  de  Periculo  Maris),  cf.  0. 
Oliver's  Monasticon,  pp.  31-32,  414. 

He  seized  a  portion  of  the  possessions  of  the  Priory 
of  St.  Petrock,  Bodmin  and  converted  them  to  his  own 
use. 

He  possessed  two  hundred  and  forty  eight  manors 
in  Cornwall  and  the  castles  of  Dunhevet  (Launceston) 
and  Trematon.

ROGERS,  Thomas,  b.  Helston  1792.  Admitted 
Attorney  Easter  term  1814,  formerly  head 
partner  in  Rogers  and  Son,  solicitors,  Hel- 
ston;  Mayor  of  Helston;  Now  (Mch.  1876) 
resident  at  Helston.  m.  Jane,  dau.  of  Henry 
Trevenen  Trengrouse. 

Note.— At  the  Helston  election  30  Apl.  1866,  the 
two  candidates,  Will.  Baliol  Brett  Q.C.  (now  one  of  the 
Judges  of  the  Court  of  Common  Pleas,)  and  Eobert 
Campbell  each  polled  153  votes,  whereupon  the  Return- 
ing Officer  and  Mayor  of  Helston  Mr.  Thomas  Rogers, 
although  he  had  already  voted,  gave  a  casting  vote  in 
favour  of  the  second  candidate  and  declared  him  to  be 
duly  elected.  The  matter  being  brought  before  a  com- 
mittee of  the  house  of  commons  on  the  5  July,  it  de- 
cided 1.  That  the  rev.  Isaac  Rogers,  Wesleyan  Minister 
who  had  voted  for  Mr.  Campbell,  was  not  entitled  to  a 
vote  and  that  therefore  there  was  a  majority  in  favour 
of  Mr.  Brett.  2.  That  a  returning  officer  cannot  give  a 
casting  vote.  This  case  was  frequently  referred  to  in 
The  Elections  Eetuming  Officers  Bill  which  was  brought 
into  parliament  in  the  same  session,  cf.  T.  C.  Hansard's 
Pari.  Debates  clxxxiv,  716-17,  1075-78,  1541-44  (1866); 
The  Times  6  July  1866,  p.  6,  col.  2,  19  July,  p.  6,  cols.

De  Romfort's  Soliloquy.  Supposed  to  besp.oken 
on  the  day  previous  to  his  execution.  In  "  The

An  address  to  two  criminals  under  sentence 
of  death,   ib.  pp.  187-88. 

Note  . — The  second  criminal  was  an  innkeeper  named 
Wyatt  of  Fowey  who  had  been  convicted  of  murdering 
a  Jew.  Wyatt  was  respited  for  sometime,  a  point  of 
law  raised  in  his  favour  having  been  carried  before  the 
judges  in  banco,  cf.  James  Picciotto's  Sketches  of  Anglo- 
Jewish  Hist.  (1875)  pp.  286-87.  Both  the  poems  were 
written  by  Tobias  Martin.

La  Eoehe  a  Prenoh  prisoner.  For  having  forged  and

EOSCADDON,  Mr.  Went  on  a  pilgrimage; 
Left  his  estate  by  will,  in  trust  to  John  Tre- 
gosse  of  St.  Ives  for  the  benefit  of  his  wife 
and  children,  but  J.  Tregosse  taking  advan- 
tage of  an  informality  in  the  deed,  took  pos- 
session of  the  lands  etc.  and  turned  the  wife 
and  children  out  of  doors  to  starve,  cf.  Rev. 
Samuel  Clarke's  Lives  of  sundry  eminent  persons 
(1683)  Life  of  Thomas  Tregosse,  p.  109.

God's  vengeance  upon  the  rebels  in  Ireland, 
being  A  true  relation  how  upon  the  7  day  of 
December  they  slew  two  and  twenty  of  the  pro- 
testant  shepheards  and  drove  away  the  greater 
part  of  their  cattell.  Also  how  upon  the  8  day 
of  December,  the  rebels  having  made  themselves 
drunke,  afterward  each  man  slew  his  friend,  to 
the  number  of  three  thousand,  it  being  the 
birth-day  of  the  Great  Lord  Don  Marketodough, 
chiefe  rebell  in  Ireland.  Likewise  how  the  pro- 
testants  ceazed  upon  their  cattell  and  other  spoile 
to  the  great  overthrow  of  all  the  rebels  in  Ire- 
land. Sent  in  a  Letter  from  Colonell  Eouse  to 
Sir  lohn  Sampson,  knight  and  baronet,  Deoemb. 
14,  1641,  4".  Lond.  printed  for  William  Bow- 
den  1641,  4°-  4  leaves.

Note. — The  second  wife  of  Sir  Anthony  Eous  was

admitted  to  the  Inner  Temple,  Francis  Eous  "  his  re- 
lation "  became  his  surety  and  that  at  Pym's  funeral

Dart's  Hist,  of  Wfstminster  (1742)  ii,  146  where  Pym  is 
called  a  Cornish  Gentleman.

Note. — A.  Rowe  was  an  annual  contributor  to  the 
two  first  named  works  from  1767  to  1811  sending  calcu- 
lations of  times  of  solar  and  lunar  eclipses  and  transits 
of  stars  for  the  meridian  of  Penzance,  Mathematical 
questions  and  replies,  Eebuses  and  replies  etc.  The 
majority  of  his  Mathematical  questions  were  reprinted 
in  the  last  named  work.

Note. — G.  Bowe  has  been  sub-editor  of  "  The  Cor- 
nish Telegraph"  from  1851-61  and  from  1871  to  the 
present  time.  He  has  also  been  sub-editor  of  the  "Pen- 
zance Tidings "  from  its  commencement  in  1870.  In 
the  former  paper,  besides  other  very  numerous  articles 
he  has  published

A  report  of  the  trial.... Eowe  v.  Brenton,  etc. 
1830,  8".    See  Concanen  George. 

Note. — This  was  an  action  to  try  the  right  to  copper 
ore  raised  from  East  Crinnis  mines  near  St.  Austell, 
The  original  case  was  an  action  brought  in  1821  by 
Joshua  Eowe  against  "William  Brenton ;  the  ease  of 
Eowe  against  George  Grenfell  as  the  purchaser  of  the 
ores  was  first  heard  in  1824.  The  action  of  Eowe  v. 
Brenton  in  1830  was  brought  by  Alexander  Snow  Bowe 
as  one  of  the  executors  of  his  deceased  father,  and  re- 
sulted in  a  verdict  for  the  defendant  after  a  trial  lasting 
seven  days.  Mr.  Alexander  Francis  Snow  Eowe,  third 
son  of  Joshua  Bowe  was  killed  by  a  fall  from  his  horse 
at  Bideford  28  Feb.  1832,  aged  28.

Defoe,  headed  "  Eemarkable  passage  of  an  apparition 
1665,"  occupies  pp.  20-33.  It  was  inserted  by  C.  S.  Gil- 
bert in  his  Survey  of  Cornwall  (1817)  i,  115-19  as  an 
original  and  inedited  document  by  Mr.  Euddell.  On  this 
"  Remarkable  passage  of  an  apparition  1665,"  as  found 
in  Gilbert's  work,  Mrs.  Bray  founded  a  considerable  part 
of  her  romance  "  Trelawney  of  Trelawne."  cf.  Will.  Lee's 
Daniel  Defoe,  his  life  (18G9)  i,  pp.  xliv,  322-27  ;  N.  and 
Q.  3  S.  X,  417-18  (1866) ;  Rev.  J.  M.  Neale's  The  un- 
seen world  (1858)  pp.  112-16;  Bev.  R.  S.  Bawher's 
"  Footprints  of  former  men  in  far  Cornwall,"  (1870)  pp. 
103-24  under  the  title  of  "  The  Botathen  ghost;"  Rev.  F. 
O.  Lee's  Glimpses  of  the  supernatural  (1875)  pp.  59-69. 
It  appears  that  the  term  "the  fanatical  minister  of 
Launceston"  applied  to  Mr.  Euddell  in  some  printed 
works  is  entirely  unmerited.

Case  before  The  Chancellor  2  July  1777,  St. 
Aubyn  v.  Mr.  Harris  of  Bow  church  yard  and 
others,  cf.  Gent.  Mag.  xlvii,  348  (1777);  Annual 
'Register  XX,  192  (1777);  The  London  Evening 
Post  3  July  1777,  p.  3,  col.  2. 

Note. — Sir  J.  St.  Aubyn  being  at  Westminster 
school  in  1775  and  aged  17  procured  a  schoolfellow 
Eobert  Baker  to  join  him  in  a  bond  for  monies  advanced 
to  supply  his  extravagances.  He  now  pleaded  that  he 
■was  under  age  and  did  not  understand  the  exact  nature 
of  the  securities  which  he  gave.  The  Chancellor  order- 
ed that  all  monies  actually  advanced  should  be  repaid, 
with  4  per  cent,  interest,  aitter  which  the  bonds  were  to 
be  given  up  to  plaintiff.  The  suit  was  instituted  by  the 
Dowager  Lady  St.  Aubyn  on  the  part  of  her  son  who 
was  abroad.

SAMBLE,  EioHARD.    (Con.). 

Richard  Samble's  Testimony  concerning  Chris- 
topher Bacon  who  deceased  the  29th  day  of  the 
10th  month  1678.  Written  at  Falmouth  in  the 
county  of  Cornwall,  n.p.  or  d.  or  printer  s  name 
[1678]  4".  pp.  4. 

Note. — The  title  is  from  the  heading  of  page  i. 
This  tract  is  signed  "  Eiohard  Samble,"  and  the  final 
sentence  reads  "  The  truth  of  this  is  likewise  testified 
by  Thomas  Gwin,  Jninor."  (sic.) 

A  testimony  to  the  plainness  &  simplicity 
of  the  truth  as  it  is  in  Jesus  With  something 
concerning  baptism  and  the  supper  of  the  Lord, 
with  a  discovery  of  the  antieut  foundation  of 
faith  and  hope  ;  with  a  warning  to  all  rebellious 
people  to  turn  to  the  Lord  before  the  day  of 
sore  calamity  come  upon  them.  Also  a  tender 
invitation  unto  all  that  feel  desires  in  their 
hearts  after  the  Lord  to  come  to  the  living  sub- 
stance, out  of  all  the  figures  and  shadows.  With 
a  warning  to  all  that  despise  and  vilifie  the  light, 
that  they  may  cease  from  their  hard  speeches 
against  it,  lest  they  fall  into  that  great  sin 
which  is  unpardonable,  Matth.  3.  [verses]  2,  3. 
Written  by  one  who  after  a  long  travail  in  sor- 
row through  the  darkness  of  the  night  of  apos- 
tacy,  is  by  the  arm  of  God's  salvation  brought 
to  the  dawning  of  the  day  of  the  Lord,  which 
is  glad-tidings  to  his  soul,  called  by  the  name  of 
Eichard  Samble... Printed  in  the  year  1679,  sm. 
4°.  Title  1  leaf,  A  testimony  etc.  pp.  3-70. 

A  handful  after  the  harvest-men,  'or  a  loving 
salutation  to  Sion's  mourners,  being  a  collection 
of  several  epistles  and  testimonies  of  that  faith- 
ful labourer  in  the  Lord's  vine-yard  Eiohard 
Samble.  Together  with  several  testimonies  of 
Friends  that  were  witnesses  of  his  labour  in  the 
gospel  in  his  latter  days.  Lond.  printed  and 
sold  by  Andrew  Sowle  at  the  Crooked-Billet  in 
HoUoway  lane,  Shoreditch  1684,  4".  13  sheets. 

jjoTK. — Contains  testimonies  by  Thomas  Salthouse, 
John  ShOlson,  John  Peters,  John  Scantlebury  and  John 
Tregellas,  Jane  Samble,  William  Trethowan,  Anne  New- 
man, Edward  Sealing,  Stephen  Richards,  Abigal  Shep- 
herd, unpaged ;  A  letter  of  love  to  the  true  begotten 
children  (signed  Richard  Samble,  written  in  Swansey  in

SAWDIE,  Thomas. 

A  return  of  prayer,  or  a  faithful  relation  of 
some  remarkable  passages  of  providence  concern- 
ing Thomas  Sawdie,  a  boy  of  twelve  years  of 
age,  servant  to  lohn  Roberts  of  Trebitian  in  the 
parish  of  Lawrack  [Landrake]  and  County  of 
Cornwall,  who  was  possest  with  an  unclean  spirit 
and  through  mercy  by  prayer  and  fasting  dispos- 
sest  and  delivered  from  the  servitude  and  jaws 


SAWME,  Thomas.    (Con.). 

of  Satan.  Attested  by  several  eminent  ministers 
of  Christ  and  other  christian  friends  and  neigh- 
bours, whose  names  are  annext  to  the  ensuing 
relation.    Printed  in  the  year  1664,  sm.  4". 

Note.— Title  1  leaf,  "  The  prey  plucked  out  of  the 
mouth  of  the  lion,  or  an  account  of  some  passages  of 
providence  concerning  Thomas  Sawdie  &c."  pp.  1-22. 
Thomas  bawdie  on  returning  from  Lawrack  fair  29  June 
1663  was  met  by  a  woman  in  white  who  offered  him 
money  and  afterwards  sent  him  the  money  by  a  great 
black  dog.  The  boy  soon  after  fell  into  fits  and  was  at- 
tended by  Mr.  Gary  a  physician  of  Lyskerd  and  by  one 
Condy  of  Stoke-Clymsland,  the  latter  of  whom  said  the 
boy  was  overlookt.  The  four  ministers  who  prayed  with 
and  for  the  boy  were  Mr.  Toms,  Mr.  Lydston,  [See  ante 
i,  329]  Mr.  Travers  and  Mr.  Teag.  The  other  persons 
mentioned  are  John  Roberts  the  boy's  master  and  Eliza- 
beth his  wife,  Dorothy  Sawdie,  his  mother,  Mrs.  Jane 
Brooking  and  Mrs.  Isabella  her  daughter,  Thomas 
Geffery,  Samuel  Geffery  and  Zenobia  his  wife,  Henry 
Palmer,  constable,  Bichard  Boberts,  Josias  Lee,  Con- 
stance Record  and  Christopher  Earle.

SCOFFERN,  John  (son  of  Will.  Scoffern,  b. 
1783,  d.  1854,  who  rn.  JVilmot  Crocker,  b.  Drux- 
ton,  Devon  circa  1772,  d.  Ilford,  Essex,  Apl. 
1864).  6.  Dutson,  St.  Stejihen's-by-Launceston 
9  Oct.  1814.  hapt.  St.  Stephen's.  Educated 
by  rev.  John  Couch  Grylls,  Saltash ;  at  Al- 
dersgate  street  coll.  and  at  University  coll. ; 
Graduate  in  Medicine  of  the  University  of 
Lond.  M.B.  1843;  L.S.A.  1837;  Registered  23 
Dec.  1865  ;  Professor  of  chemistry  at  the  Al- 
dersgate  street  College  of  Medicine,  Oct. 
1840,  and  joint  Professor  of  Forensic  Medi- 
cine with  Sir  Will.  Hodges ;  Lecturer  at  the 
Medical  school,  Charlotte  street ;  Special  Cor- 
respondent of  a  Morning  paper  at  the  com- 
mencement of  the  Franco-Prussian  war ;  At- 
tached to  the  2nd  company  5tli  Silesian  Jagers 
at  the  advanced  posts ;  Was  the  only  civihan 
in  the  palace  of  St.  Cloud  when  it  was  bom- 
barded and  burnt  by  the  French  13  Oct.  1870; 
Received  the  Iron'Cross  of  Prussia  for  bravery 
and  care  of  sick  and  wounded,  Oct.  1871,  the 
only  civilian  who  has  been  so  rewarded,  the 
fact  is  recorded  on  a  tablet  in  the  Jager  garri- 
son at  Gorlitz.  Resident  (June  1876)  at  21 
Appleford  road,  Upper  Westbourne  park, 
London,  m.  See  Scoffern,  J.  F. 

Note. — Mr.  John  Scoffern  is  called  on  some  of  his 
title  pages  J.  B.  Scoffern  and  J.  G.  Scoffern  respectively, 
these  names  are  printers'  errors  as  he  has  bnt  one 
christian  name.  He  has  also  been  erroneously  described 
as  John  Douglas  Scoffern.  The  afdx  of  F.S.A.  to  his 
name  is  likewise  a  mistake  as  he  is  not  a  member  of 
the  Society  of  Antiquaries.

Avon  23  Apl.  1564.  d.  Stratford  23  Apl. 
1616. 

The  London  prodigal.  As  it  was  plaide  by 
the  Kings  Maiesties  seruants.  By  William 
Shakespeare.  Lond.  printed  byT.C.  for  Nathan- 
iel Butter  1605,  4".  A  to  G  in  fours.— .Reprint- 
ed. Lond.  printed  in  the  year  1709,  4". — .Re- 
printed. Lond.  printed  for  J.  Tonson  1734,  12". 
pp.  130. 

Note. — One  of  the  principal  characters  in  this 
play  is  "Oliver,  a  Cornish  clothier  in  love  with  Luce  " 
who  speaks  in  what  is  presumed  to  be  intended  for  the 
Cornish  dialect.  This  play  which  is  generally  numbered 
amongst  the  spurious  plays  attributed  to  Shakspeare  is 
also  found  in  the  following  collections  : — 

The  works  of  W.  Shakespear.  [By  Alexander  Pope 
and  Geo.  Sewell].  Lond.  J.  Tonson  1728,  Vol.  ix,  pp. 
73-130. 

Supplement  to  the  editions  of  Shakspeare's  plays 
published  in  1778.  By  Samuel  Johnson  and  George 
Steevens.  [By  B.  Malone].  Lond.  1780,  2  vols.  S".  ii, 
447-529. 

A  supplement  to  the  plays  of  W.  Shakspeare.  By 
W.  G.  Simms,  Esg.    New  York  1848,  8».  pp.  45-65. 

In  Twelfth  night,  act  iii,  scene  1,  is  the  following 
reference  to  pilchards  :  "  And  fools  are  as  like  husbands, 
as  pilchards  are  to  herrings,  the  husband's  the  bigger. "

SMITH,  Augustus  John  (2nd  son  of  Jas.  Smith 
h.  3  Sep.  1768,  d.  Ashlyn  Hall,  Herts  16  Feb.

sident of  the  E.G.S.C.  1856-64;  President

Freemason  v,  489-90  (1872).

Scilly,  from  1834  to  1847. . .Lond.  W.  E.  Painter, 
342  Strand  1848,  8».

Note. — Berkhampstead  common  in  the  coujities  of 
Hertford,  Buckingham  and  Northampton  formerly  be- 
longed to  the  duchy  of  Cornwall  but  was  purchased  by 
earl  Brownlow  in  1862.  In  Feb.  1866  the  earl  enclosed 
about  400  acres  with  an  iron  fence  which  Augustus 
Smith  acting  as  a  freeholder  and  copyhold  tenant  of 
the  manor  and  accompanied  by  a  gang  of  navvies  for- 
cibly tore  down  and  removed.  The  earl  commenced  an 
action  against  A.  Smith,  but  dying  soon  after,  the 
action  could  not  be  proceeded  with.  In  July  1866  the 
latter  filed  a  bill  against  the  then  earl  Brownlow,  the 
case  was  heard  in  the  KoUs  Court  15,  18  Nov.  1869  and 
14  Jan.  1870  when  A.  Smith  got  an  injunction  pre- 
venting any  future  enclosure  of  the  common,  cf.  The 
Law  Times  Reports  xxi,  739-42  (1870) ;  TJie  Lmo  Times 
xlviii,  229-30,  263-64  (1870) ;  Punch  1,  125  (1866).

Cornwall "  pp.  i-ii,  List  of  subscribers  pp.  i-xxvii.  Life 
reviewed  pp.  1-70,  An  elegy  pp.  71-86.  There  are  ten 
notes  containing  biographical  notices  of  persons  men- 
tioned in  the  text.  The  list  of  Subscribers  is  one  of 
the  most  extensive  to  be  found  in  any  book  relating  to 
the  county,  it  comprises  upwards  of  2000  names,  a  very 
large  proportion  being  those  of  residents  in  Cornwall 
and  Devon.

SMITH,  Rev.  George  Charles,  h.  Castle 
street,  Leicester  square,  London  19  March 
1782.  Apprentice  to  a  bookseller,  Tooley  st. 
London  1794-96;  Apprenticed  to  the  Master 
of  an  American  brig  1796;  Pressed  into 
H.M.S.  "Scipio"  at  Surinam,  West  Indies 
1796;  Then  in  H.M.S.  "Ariadne";  Midship- 
man in  H.M.S.  "Agamemnon"  1797;  Mas- 
ter's Mate;  In  battle  of  Copenhagen  2  Apl. 
1801;  Converted  at  Reading  19  Mch.  1803; 
A  student  under  rev.  Isaiah  Birt,  Baptist  min- 
ister, Devonport  1804-1807;  Pastor  of  Bap- 
tist Chapel,  Penzance  1807-25,  1848-63  and 
preacher  to  sailors  at  Penzance,  St.  Just  and 
Newlyn  ;  Introduced  the  Lancastrian  school 
system  into  France ;  Built  six  chapels  in  vil- 
lages around  Penzance  1812-16 ;  Went  on  mis- 
sion to  Duke  of  Wellington's  army  in  Spain, 
Mch.-July  1814;  Commenced  open  air  preach- 
ing in  Tavistock  Sq.  1817;  Bethel  flag  first 
used  1817;  Founder  of  the  Bethel  Union  Soc. 
1819  ;  Founder  of  the  Liverpool  Seamen's 
Friendly  Soc,  and  Bethel  Union  1821 ;  Ori- 
ginator of  the  Merchant  Seamen's  orphan 
asylum  for  boys  1823 ;  Opened  first  Mariner's 
Church,  Wellclose-square,  London  1825;  Foun- 
der of  Domestic  City  Mission  and  Asylnm 
for  starving  sailors  1824;  Founder  of  first 
Temperance  Soc.  in  England  1828;  Founder 
of  Maritime  Female  refuge  1829 ;  Visited 
New  York  May-July  1861.  d.  Jordan  house, 
Penzance  10  Jan.  1863.  hur.  The  cemetery 
1 6  Jan.  cf.  The  British  Standard  (Ed.  hy  Dr. 
Oamphell)  vii,  21  col.  3,  29  col.  6,  38  col.  3; 
Qent.  Mag.  xiv,  260,  390-91  (1863);  The 
Cornish  Telegraph  2  Deo.  1868;  Tlie  Congrega- 
tional Year  Book  for  1862,  p.  223 ;  The  Baptist 
Mag.  xl,  293,  563,  690  (1848);  Bev.  F.  A. 
Cox's  Ordination  Services  (1820),  pp.  41-47.

Note. — Six  numbers  printed  only,  the  pagination 
extends  from  1  to  96.  The  Brunswick  theatre,  a  new 
house,  was  opened  25  Feb.  1828,  on  the  28  Feb.  between 
11  and  12  during  a  rehearsal  of  "  Guy  Mannering  "  the 
roof  fell  in  killing  11  people  and  dangerously  wounding 
24  others. 

The  ground  of  the  theatre,  an  interesting  re- 
view of  circumstances  connected  with  the  ground 
upon  which  the  late  "Royalty"  and  the  more 
recent  New  Brunswick  theatre  stood... with  pro- 
posals for  restoring  the  ground  back  to  its  origi- 
nal appropriation  by  erecting  a  building  that 
shall  be  devoted  to  purposes  of  religion  and 
benevolence.  By  G.  C.  Smith.  Lond.  W.  K. 
Wakefield  [6  Mch.]  1828,  12°.  3d.

SMITH,  William. 

A  full  account  cif  the  late  ship-wreck  of  the 
ship  called  the  President,  which  was  cast  away 
in  Montz-bay  in  Cornwal  on  the  4th  of  Feb- 
ruary last,  as  it  was  deliver'd  to  his  majesty 
(both  in  writing  and  in  discourse)  by  "William 
Smith  and  John  Harshfield,  the  only  persons 
that  escaped  in  the  said  wreck.  Together  with 
all  the  remarkable  adventures,  in  the  said  voy- 
age from  their  sailing  out  of  the  sound  at  Pli- 
mouth  on  May-Day  1682,  particularly  their  en- 
gagement with  six  pirate-ships  at  once  on  the 
coast  of  Malabar.  The  whole  relation  being 
taken  in  private  conference  with  the  said  Wil- 
liam Smith.  Lond.  printed  for  Eandal  Taylor 
near  Stationers  hall  1684,  fol.  pp.  8. 

Note. — W.  Smith  and  J.  Harshfield  came  ashore 
on  a  part  of  the  bow-sprit,  at  the  Land's  End,  where 
they  were  rescued  from  the  attempted  violence  of  some 
wreckers  by  the  appearance  of  Mr.  Godolphin's  steward 
on  horseback.

STAMFOED,  [Henry  Grey]  Earl  of  (son  of 
hon.  sir  John  Chxy  and  Elizabeth,  dau.  of  lord 
Abergavenny ).  2nd  baron  Grey;  Cr.  earl  of 
Stamford  26  Mch.  1628.  One  of  the  Com- 
manders of  the  parliamentary  forces  in  Corn- 
wall 1643,  Defeated  at  the  battle  of  Stamford 
Hill  near  Stratton  16  May  1643.  d.  1673. 
cf.  Clarendon's  Hist,  of  the  rebellion  (1849)  ii, 
486,  493-96,  499,  iii,  72-77. 

27  Oct.  1643.  Complaint  of  the  earl  of  Stam- 
ford against  Mr.  Anthony  Nicholls,  M.P.  deputy 
lieutenant  of  Devon  and  Cornwall.  The  defeat 
at  Stratton  was  owing  to  NichoUs  moving  the 
horse  to  Bodmin,  and  to  the  treachery  of  major- 
general  Chudleigh,  whose  order  Nicholls  pro- 
duced for  removing  the  horse.  MS.  House  of 
Lords  vi,  278. 

30  Oct.  1643.  Eemonstrance  of  the  earl  of 
Stamford  to  the  lords  concerning  arrears  of  pay, 
etc.    ib.  vi,  284. 

27  Nov.  1643.  Further  remonstrances  of  the 
earl  of  Stamford.  A  Committee  has  been  nomin- 
ated to  enquire  into  the  charge  against  Mr. 
Nicholls.   ib.  vi,  314. 

23  Dec.  1643.  Letter  from  the  earl  of  Stam- 
ford, to  the  lords,  prays  that  the  arrears  may  be 
paid,  and  that  some  of  the  estates  of  the  delin- 
quents may  be  given  to  him.   ib.  vi,  351.

Act  4  Hen.  VIII,  c.  8,  (1512)  entitled  "  Strode's  Act,"

in  parliament,  cf.  "  The  Statutes.  Revised  ed."  (1870)

SWAINE,  Sampson. 

A.D.  1762,  May  21,  No.  774.  Specification 
of  Sampson  Swaine  of  the  parish  of  Cambrae 
(sic)  in  the  county  of  Cornwall,  smelter  and 
refiner  for  "New  method  of  constructing  and 
adapting  to  each  other  a  machine,  furnace  and 
fire  engine,  so  that  the  same  fire  should  at  the 
same  time  be  capable  of  smelting  and  refining 
several  sorts  of  metals  and  working  a  fire  engine 
to  raise  water  and  stamp  ores  and  serve  many 
other  useful  purposes  in  a  manner  not  hitherto 
put  in  practice."  Lond.  1762,  fol. — .Eeprinted.

703 


TANGYE. 


TANGYE  BEOS.  (Con.). 

Pountney  lane,  London,  and  engineers  at  Birmingham 
and  Smethwick,  consisted  of  James,  Joseph,  Edward, 
Eichard,  and  George  Tangye,  and  Stephen  Holman,  but 
Edward  Tangye  retired  1  June,  1869,  and  James  and 
Joseph  Tangye  1  Jan.  1872. 

Geo.  Jas.  Jos.  Bich.  and  Edward  Tangye  originally 
commenced  business  in  Birmingham  in  1856  ;  Manu- 
factured the  hydraulic  rams  used  for  launching  the 
Great  Eastern  1857-58 ;  Commenced  making  screw 
jacks  and  other  railway  tools  1858 ;  Bemoved  to  new 
premises  in  Clement  st.  1859;  Perfected  Mr.  T.  A. 
Weston's  Differential  pulley  block  1859  and  purchased 
the  patent  from  him  ;  Eemoved  to  new  works  at  Smeth- 
wick 1864 ;  The  first  makers  in  Europe  of  direct-acting 
steam  pumps  1867 ;  Manufacturers  of  Jas.  Tangye's 
horizontal  steam  engines  1869;  Employ  now  (Nov.  1876) 
about  1300  hands  in  the  Cornwall  works. 

TANGYE,  Edward  (son  of  Jos.  Tangye,  farmer, 
Illogan,  d.  Camborne  22  Mch.  1854,  aged  57, 
m.  Anil,  dau.  of  Edw.  Bidlock,  She  d.  Illogan 
23  Aug.  1851,  aged  51).  b.  Illogan  27  Apl. 
1832 ;  Emigrated  to  America  1855,  returned 
to  England  1858.  See  under  Tangye  Bros,  for 
further  details  of  E.  Tangye  and  his  brothers.

TAYLOR,  John.  (Con.). 

John  Taylors  wanderings  to  see  the  wonders 
of  the  west.  How  he  travelled  neere  600  miles 
from  London  to  the  Mount  in  Cornwall  and 
beyond  the  Mount  to  the  Lands  end  and  home 
againe.  Dedicated  to  all  his  loving  friends  and 
free  minded  benefactors.  In  these  dangerous  days 
for  rich  men  and  miserable  times  for  the  poore 
servants  of  the  late  king  (whereof  1  was  one 
45  years  to  his  royall  father  and  himself)  I  thought 
it  needful  to  take  some  course  to  make  use  of 
some  friends  and  devise  a  painfull  way  for  my 
subsistence ;  which  was  the  journey  I  have  past 
and  this  booke  heere  present ;  for  which  purpose 
I  gave  out  many  of  these  following  bills,  to 
which  neere  3000  gentlemen  and  others  have 
kindly  subscribed  to  give  me  a  reasonable  reward. 
Printed  in  the  yeere  1649,  sm.  4". 

Note.— Title  1  page.  The  bill  of  John  Taylor  1  page, 
Taylors  "tt'esterne  voyage  to  the  Mount,  pp.  1-21.  J. 
Taylor  was  in  Cornwall  from  the  sixth  to  the  twenty 
fourth  of  July,  1619.  This  work  has  been  reprinted  as 
foUows  : — 

Mr.  E.  W.  Ashbee's  Occasional  Fac-simile  reprints. 
Limited  to  100  copies  of  each.  No.  Yiii  Wanderings  to 
see  the  wonders  of  the  west.  By  John  Taylor  .  .  .  Lond. 
printed  for  subscribers  only,  1869,  4". 

Publications  of  the  Spencer  Society  [Manchester.] 
Issue  No.  7.  Works  of  John  Taylor  .  .  .  First  collection. 
Printed  for  the  Spenser  Society  1870,  4°.  Contains  fac- 
simile reprint  of  Wanderings  to  see  the  wonders  of  the 
west.

Charles  Hindley,  esq.  London  and  Westminster,  Beeves 
and  Turner  1872,  4°.  Contains  Wanderings  to  see  the 
wonders  of  the  west.

west.  .  .  In  "  Cornish  Telegraph  "  12  and  19  Oct.  1864,

A  valorous  and  perillous  sea-fight.  Fought 
with  three  Turkish  ships,  pirats  or  men  of  warre, 
on  the  coast  of  Cornewall  (or  westerne  part  of 
England)  by  the  good  ship  named  the  EHzabeth 
of  Plimmouth,  she  being  of  the  burthen  of  200 
tuns,  which  fight  was  bravely  fought  on  Wed- 
nesdaiy,  the  17  of  lune  last  past,  1640.  Loud, 
printed  by  E.  P.  for  Edward  Wright,  dwelling 
neer  Christs-church  gate  1640,  sm.  4". 

Note.— Woodcut  of  the  Elizabeth  1  leaf.  Title 
1  leaf,  A  valorovs  sea-fight  4  leaves.  The  examination 
of  John  Whiddon  -of  Plimmouth  3  pages,  A  friendly, 
true,  deserved  commendation  and  encouragement  of  all 
mariners,  signed  "John  Taylor "8  pages.  This  pam- 
phlet was  reprinted  in  The  Comish  Telegraph  10  May, 
1864.  The  fight  took  place  "  within  two  leagues  neere 
to  the  Lizzard,  which  is  a  small  island,  a  good  sea  marke 
on  the  west  part  of  the  Mount  in  Cornwall  between 
Scilly  and  the  main."

THOMAS,  William  Millett,  Truro. 

The  case  of  Vice  against  Thomas. ,  .By  Edward 
Smirke  1843  q.v.  ante  ii,  658. 

Note. — W.  M.  Thomas  holding  certain  tin  bounds, 
W.  Vice  and  J.  Bennetts  entered  on  and  took  posBBB- 
sion  of  the  same ;  being  summoned  before  the  vice  warden 
they  filed  a  demurrer,  but  the  demurrer  was  overmled 
with  costs  and  the  defendants  were  ordered  to  file  their 
answer  to  the  petition.  From  this  judgment  they  appealed 
and  the  case  was  argued  before  his  royal  highness  the 
lord  warden  who  had  called  to  his  assistance  lord  Lynd- 
hurst,  lord  Brougham,  lord  Langdale,  vice  chancellor 
Wigram,  and  baron  Parke,  when  after  long  arguments, 
the  order  was  reversed  without  costs.

The  eccentric  and  singular  productions  of  sir 
W  Courtenay,  K.M.  alias  Mr.  Tom,  spirit  mer- 
chant and  maltster  of  Truro  in  Cornwall,  late 
candidate  for  the  representation  of  the  city  of 
Canterbury  in  parliament;  now  an  inmate  of  the 
lunatic  asylum,  Barm.ing  heath  near  Maidstone. 
To  which  are  added  his  weekly  publication  "  The 
Lion"  from  the  first  number  to  its  conclusion. 
The  whole  accurately  reprinted  as  issued  by  him 
during  and  after  the  election.  His  trial  at  Maid- 
stone for  perjury,  on  Thursday  July  the  26th, 
1833  before  Mr.  Justice  James  Parke,  with  other 
interesting  information  never  before  published.

this  extraordinary  individual  both  as  Mr.  N.  Tom 
and  sir  William  Courtenay,  K.M.,  and  a  neatly 
executed  print  representing  the  High  street,  Can- 
terbury with  the  knight  addressing  the  public 
from  the  balcony  of  the  Rose  inn...Canterbui7, 
printed  and  published  by  Henry  Ward... sold  in

The  trial  pp.  34-39,  Conclusion  p.  40.

The  life  and  extraordinary  adventures  of  sir 
WilHam  Courtenay,  knight  of  Malta  alias  John 
Nichols  Tom,  formerly  spirit  merchant  and  mal- 
ster  of  Truro  in  Cornwall,  being  a  correct  detail 
of  all  the  incidents  of  his  extraordinary  life, 
from  his  infancy  to  the  dreadful  battle  at  Bos- 
senden  wood,  illustrated  by  numerous  engra- 
vings of  the  principal  scenes  of  that  dreadful 
tragedy,  with  fac-similes  of  the  autographs  of 
that  eccentric  character,  concluding  with  an  ac- 
curate account  of  the  trial  of  the  rioters  at  the 
Maidstone  assizes.   By  Canterburiensis  [jmud. 

i.e. ].  Canterbury,  published  by  James  Hunt,

An  alphabetical  account  of  all  the  parishes  in 
Cornwall,  the  1st  volume  A- J  brought  down  to 
theyearl702  by  William  Halse  of  Fenton  Gymps, 
gent,  with  large  additions  and  amendments  to 
the  whole  and  brought  down  to  1736  by  Thos. 
Tonkin  of  Trevennance.  The  2nd  volume  K-Z 
[but  in  fact  only  completed  to  Otterham]  com- 
pleted wholly  and  brought  down  to  the  sd.  year 
1736  by  the  sd.  Thomas  Tonkin.  MSS.  2  vols. 
sm.  4°.  on  vellum. 

Note. — The  above  volumes  were  purchased  by  the 
late  Mr.  'William  Sandys  from  Thomas  Kerslake,  book- 
seller, Bristol  in  Apl.  1856,  after  Mr.  Sandys'  decease  in 
1874  they  passed  into  the  possession  of  the  present 
owner  "W.  C.  Borlase  esq.  of  Castlehorneck.  The  first  vol. 
contains  pp.  612,  the  second  pp.  414  but  several  of  these 
ai'e  blank.  There  is  a  letter  at  the  commencement  called 
an  Advertisement  much  shorter  than  and  different  from 
that  given  in  De  Dunstanville's  Carew,  complaining  of 
Hals'  ignorance  and  of  his  ill-conduct,  it  then  goes  on  to 
say  "  I  shall  only  add  that  whatever  is  enclosed  in  these 
lines  [  ]  are  my  notes  or  additions  or  amendments  as 
may  be  easily  seen  and  that  all  which  follows  at  the  end 
of  his  accts.  of  every  parish,  under  the  long  lines  thus 

are  my  own  observations  wholly  by  way  of  addition 

to  his.  I  am  going  on  as  fast  as  my  leisure  will  permit 
with  the  2nd  volume  from  K  to  Z  and  whatever  faults  are 
in  that  must  be  laid  to  my  score  only.  Let  me  but  add 
two  or  three  words  more,  that  as  I  design  this  but  as  a 
common  place  book  in  order  to  a  more  correct  survey  of 
Cornwall,  whatever  the  fate  of  these  papers  may  be,  they 
are  by  no  means  fit  to  be  published  as  they  are.  Tho. 
Tonkin,  Trevaunance  anno  domini  1715."  These  books 
seem  to  be  the  M8.  or  Common  place  books  above  re- 
ferred to.  The  first  volume  comprises  the  copy  of  the 
collections  of  Hals  probably  up  to  1702  when  the  inter- 
course between  Tonkin  and  Hals  seems  to  have  ceased 
and  the  bracketed  portions  and  additions  referred  to  by 
Tonkin  appear  to  a  considerable  extent.  Hals  added 
considerably  to  his  collections  after  1702  which  ac- 
counts for  this  MS.  not  containing  many  things  that 
afterwards  appeared  in  Hals.  The  second  volume  com- 
prises Tonkin's  MS.  only  according  to  the  title  of  the 
MS.  and  stops  at  Otterham. 

The  portion  of  Tonkin's  MS.  histoi7  now  in  the 


TONKIN,  Thomas.    (Con.). 

Librm-y  of  the  R.I.C.  at  Truro  (cf.  Journ.  R.I.G.  Apl. 
1874,  pp.  xxv-xxvi.)  after  passing  through  various  hands 
was  given  by  the  rev.  F.  W.  Pye,  S.  of  Blisland  to  that 
Institution.  It  is  a  continuation  of  the  MSS.  possessed 
by  Mr.  Borlase  and  consists  of  four  parts  stitched  to- 
gether containing  an  account  of  the  parishes  from  Pad- 
stow  to  Zennar,  the  parishes  are  numbered  146  to  203 
and  the  pagination  extends  from  406  to  610  but  in  the 
beginning  of  the  first  part  there  are  two  or  three  leaves 
not  paged.  This  MS.  was  at  one  time  lent  to  the  rev. 
John  'Whitaker,  who  supposing  that  the  loan  was  a  gift, 
made  some  memoranda  on  the  MS.,  which  he  how- 
ever "took  care  to  erase"  before  he  returned  it.  The 
cancelled  parts  have  however  been  printed  by  Davies 
Gilbert.  Two  small  portions  appear  nevertheless  to 
have  been  retained  by  "Whitaker  namely  one  portion  of 
8  pages  relating  to  St.  Piran  in  the  Sands  paged  460a, 
460b,  460c,  460d.  The  second  portion  consists  of  4 
pages  on  foolscap  octavo  relating  to  St.  Ewe  and  the 
inscriptions  on  the  grave  stones  there  &c.,  this  not 
being  uniform  with  the  original,  possibly  did  not  be- 
long to  it.  The  two  small  portions  spoken  of  are  now 
(Jan.  1877)  penes  Sir  John  Maclean  at  Bicknor  Court, 
Coleford,  Gloucestershire. 

Copy  of  a  portion  of  Thomas  Tonkin's  History 
of  Cornwall.  Br.  Museum  Addit.  MSS.  29,  763,

TRAPNEL,  Anna  (dau.  of  Will.  Trapnel,  ship- 

Wright  of  Poplar,  London). 

Anna  Trapnel's  Report  and  plea,  or  a  narra- 
tive of  her  journey  from  London  into  Cornwall 
the  occasion  of  it,  the  Lord's  encouragements  to 
it,  and  signal  presence  with  her  in  it.  Proclaim- 
ing the  rage  and  strivings  of  the  people  against 
the  comings  forth  of  the  Lord  Jesus  to  reign. 

Manifested  in  the  harsh,  rough,  boisterous 

usage  of  Anna  Trapnel,  by  the  justices  and 
people  in  Cornwal,  at  a  place  called  Truro.  Print- 
ed at  London  for  Thomas  Brewster,  at  the  Three 
Bibles,  neer  London  house  1654,  4".  8i  leaves.

The  ancient  and  modern  history  of  Falmouth

dennis  castle,  St.  Mawes  castle,  borough  of  St.

men's seats  in  the  vicinity  &c.  Compiled  by

the  author,  sold  also  by  the  different  booksellers; 
Longman  and  Co.  London  and  all  others  1815, 
8".  pp.  86.

TEEFFEY,  Joseph  Thomas.     (Con.). 

Fowey  6  Oct.  1748,  d.  Place  9  Dec.  1842).  bapt. 
Plymouth  1  May  1782;  Assumed  the  sur- 
name of  Treffry  in  place  of  Austen  by  royal 
warrant  14  Feb.  1838;  Sheriff  of  Cornwall 
1838;  Silver  lead  smelter,  shipowner,  mer- 
chant and  farmer;  Constructor  and  proprietor 
of  the  ports  of  Par  and  Newquay ;  Designer  of 
the  railway  connecting  the  English  and  Bris- 
tol channels;  Rebuilt  Place,  d.  Place,  Fowey 
29  Jan.  1850.  iur.  Fowey  5  Feb.  JFill  dated  9 
Jan.  1841.  cf.  Gent.  Mag.  xxxiii,  433-34  (1850); 
JFest  Briton,  1  &  8  Feb.  1850;  The  Scilly  is- 
lands.  By  G.  0.  Smith  (1828)  pp.  42,  71. 

Note. — The  mines  and  other  works  with  which  J. 
T.  Treffry  was  connected  gave  occupation  to  upwards  of 
3,000  persons.  Messrs.  Lysons  in  their  "  History  of 
Cornwall"  (1814)  pp.  Ixxiv,  6,  25,  56,  108  etc.  frequently 
allude  to  J.  T.  Trefiry  as  "an  ingenious  correspondent."

TREFFRY,  Rev.  Richard,  Junior  (second  son 
of  rev.  Rich.  Treffry,  Senior,  d.  1842,  and  his 
first  2vife  Jane  Hawkey  d.  1829).  b.  Camel- 
ford  30  Nov.  1804;  Educ.  at  Kingswood  sch. 
1812-17,  Sep.  1818  to  1819;  Resident  at 
Falmouth  1817;  A  compositor  in  a  printing 
office  in  London  Mch.  1820  to  1821;  Classi- 
cal tutor  in  an  academy  at  Rochester  1822; 
Preached  his  first  sermon  near  Chatham  Apl. 
1823;  Itinerant  preacher  in  Sevenoaks  circuit 
Mch.  1824  to  1825;  At  Beverley  1825,  AtNot- 
tingham  1826,  In  Southwark  1827;  Ordained 
Wesl.  minister  1828;  At  York  1829,  At  Bram- 
ley  1830;  Invalided  at  Penzance  Feb.  1831 
to  his  decease,  i^.  Penzance  20  Jan,  1838.  bur. 
St.  Mary's  chapel-yard  26  Jan.  m.  June  1828

Hacky  &  Marky,  being  the  adventures  of 
Hacky  Daniel  and  Marky  Retchatts,  two  Cor- 
nish miners  who  went  further  to  sea  than  they

TEEGELLES,  Samuel  Prideaux  (son  of  Sam. 
Tregelles,  d.  1828  q.v.,  m.  Dorothy,  dau  of  George 
Prideaux  d.  1873.  q.v.).  b.  Wodehouse  place, 
Falmouth  30  Jan.  1813.  Educ.  at  Falmouth 
classical  sch.  under  rev.  Thos.  Sheepshanks 
1825-28;  Employed  in  the  iron  works  Neath 
abbey,  Glamorganshire  1828-34;  Brought  up 
as  a  Friend;  Joined  the  Plymouth  Brethren; 
Attended  the  Presbyterian  ch.  Plymouth; 
Took  his  first  journey  on  the  Continent  to 
collate  MSS.  Oct.  1845;  Resided  in  Rome 
Nov.  1845-Apl.  1846  to  collate  the  Vatican 
MS.  No.  1209  (B),  but  was  not  permitted  to 
transcribe  any  part  of  it ;  Went  abroad  several 
times  from  1849-62;  LL.D.  of  the  Univ.  of 
St.  Andrews  1850;  Hon.  Member  of  the  Ply- 
mouth Instit.  1850;  Received  Civil  list  pen- 
sion of  £100  per  annum  1862  to  which,  in 
consequence  of  his  illness,  another  £100  was 
added  in  1870;  Invited  to  join  the  Revisers  of 
the  authorized  version  of  the  Bible  1870,  but 
did  not  join  them ;  Paralysed  and  unable  to 
work  1870-75.  d.  6  Portland  sq.  Plymouth  24 
Apl.  1875.  hir.  Plymouth  cemetery  29  Apl. 
m.  Sarah  Anna  Tregelles.  q.v.  cf  Trans.  Ply- 
mouth Instit.  V,  386-89  (1875)  by  J.  B.  R[owe]; 
JFestern  Morning  News  29  Afl.  1875  ;  N.  and 
Q.  4  S.  iv,  383  (1869);  Scrivener's  Introduction 
to  criticism  of  Neio  Testame.nt  (1874)  pp.  102, 
429-31.

TEEGELLES,  Sarah  Anna  (eld.  dau.  of  Walter

which  are  added  the  several  acts  of  parliament,

136,  5/6. 

Note.— The  1st  ed.  is  dedicated  "To  the  right  wor- 
shipful John  Thomas  esq.,  vice  warden  of  the  stan- 
naries of  Cornwall,"  by  "  Worshipful  sir,  your  most 
obedient  servant,  J[oseph]  T[regoning],  Truro  1st  Jan. 
1808."  W.  J.  Henwood  esq.,  F.R.S.  had  the  MSS.  com- 
piled by  Dr.  Bich.  Edwards  and  J.  Tregoning  for  a  new 
edition  of  Pryce's  Mineralogia.  J.  Tregoning,  who  was 
one  of  the  earliest  mineralogists  in  Cornwall,  formed 
collections  of  minerals  for  many  of  the  county  gentle- 
men. He  also  furnished  a  large  collection  to  the  Court 
of  France.

Eeport  from  select  committee  on  Chatham 
election.  Ordered  to  be  printed  19  May  1835, 
fol.  Pari  Papers  1835,  vol.  ix. 

Note. — This  committee  sat  to  consider  a  petition 
which  stated  that  General  Tremenheere  excluded  certain 
tradesmen  from  the  Chatham  barracks  because  they  did 
not  vote  for  sir  John  Beresford  the  government  candi- 
date. The  petition  of  colonel  Tremenheere  to  the  house 
of  commons  occupies  pp.  69-70;  H.  S.  Tremenheere's 
evidence  is  on  pp.  40-41.  General  Tremenheere  success- 
fully vindicated  his  character  from  this  charge. 

TEEMENHEEEE,  Eev.  William  (eld.  son  of 
Will.  Tremenheere  atlarney,  h.  Penzance  1732, 
d.  Penzance  14  June  1780,  m.  Catharine,  6th 
dau.  of  rev.  JFalter  Borlase.  She  was  b.  Castle 
Horneck  near  Penzance  1733,  d.  Penzance  12 
Oct.  1812).  Of  Pemb.  coll.  Camb.  B.A.  1779, 
M.A.  1830;  Chaplain  E.N. ;  Chaplain  to  vis- 
count Torrington;  Succeeded  his  uncle  rev. 
William  Borlase  as  V.  of  Madron,  conformably 
to  the  will  of  his  grandfather  rev.  Walter  Bor- 
lase, and  on  the  presentation  of  Henry  Pen- 
neck,  M.D.,  executor  of  rev.  Will.  Borlase,  In- 
stituted 13  Nov.  1812,  inducted  21  Nov.,  read 
himself  in  22  Nov.,  was  vicar  to  his  decease;

bur.  Madron  where  is  Monu. ;  Unmarried,  cf.

(1838).

Note. — Mr.  Trengroupe  exhibited  his  apparatus  at 
Woohvich  28  Feb.  1818  before  admiral  sir  Chas.  Eowley, 
on  his  report  the  admiralty  had  the  intention  of  pur- 
chasing 20  sets,  but  afterwards  preferring  to  manufac- 
ture the  apparatus  in  the  ordnance  department,  they 
gave  Mr.  Trengrouse  £50  as  a  compensation  for  the  sup- 
posed amount  of  profit  he  would  have  made  on  the 
order.

Note. — By  his  will  dated  4  Nov.  1647,  he  gave  to 
the  poor  of  Eame,  2/-  weekly  for  ever.  He  also  gave  to 
the  poor  of  Muker  2/-  weekly,  and  to  the  poor  of  St. 
John's  near  Maker  1/-  weekly.  The  money  to  be  given 
in  bread  every  Sunday  morning,  and  to  be  paid  out  of 
his  manor  of  Bottleyeate.  cf.  Fifth  Bep.  of  Charity  Com- 
mission 1821,  fol.  pp.  234-35 ;  Pari.  Papers  1821,  vol.  xii,

at  Berkeley  the  vi  daye  of  Feuerer  the  yere  of  our  lorde 
mccclxxxxvii  the  yere  of  the  reyne  of  king  Eycharde  the 
fleconde  after  the  oonqueste  of  Englande  xxii.  The  yere 
of  my  lordes  age,  syre  Thomas  lorde  of  Berkeley  that 
made  me  to  make  this  translation  xlvii..."  A  MS.  in 
Berkeley  castle  says  the  translation  was  finished  18  Apl. 
1357,  the  31st  Edw.  Ill  and  in  the  65th  year  of  the  age 
of  syre  Thomas,  lord  Berkeley.

Several  authors  state  that  J.  Trevisa  made  an  Eng- 
lish translation  of  the  bible,  but  we  have  not  found  any 
reasons  for  believing  this  statement  and  no  remains  of 
the  translation  have  ever  been  discovered.

on  the  cost  of  working  the  Petersburg  and 


TEEVITHIGK,  F.  H.     (Con.). 

Moscow  railway  1861  and  1862;  Remodeled 
the  working  of  the  Homburg  railway  and  be- 
came a  director  of  the  Co.  1862;  Went  to 
Buenos  Ayres  to  report  on  the  cost  of  making 
and  working  a  railway  there  8  Oct.  1862; 
Erected  a  steam  floating  bridge  to  carry  the 
trafiic  between  Gosport  and  Portsmouth  1864; 
Manager  of  the  mechanical  engineering  de- 
partment of  Peto  and  Betts  in  England  for 
Russia,  Germany,  Denmark  and  South  America 
1861-67;  Went  to  U.S.  of  America  to  report 
on  the  Atlantic  and  G.W.  railway  1867;  Went 
to  Canada  to  report  on  the  Grand  Trunk  rail- 
way 1868;  Went  to  South  Germany  and 
Spain  to  examine  the  peat  deposits  1869; 
Purchased  and  re-established  the  Bute  Dock 
iron  foundry  1871;  Went  to  Lisbon  as  Engi- 
neer of  the  Lisbon  steam  tramway  3  Aug. 
1871;  In  connexion  with  Mr.  Hodges  intro- 
duced into  Oldenburg  the  manufacture  of  peat 
fuel  by  Mr.  Hodges'  floating  machinery  1872; 
Now  (May  1877)  resident  at  Park  hill,  Paign- 
ton, Devon,  m.  5  Mch.  1840  at  Kenwyn, 
Maria,  dau.  of  Jas.  and  Kitty  Garland.  She 
was  h.  London  13  July  1814.

Note.— When  F.  H.  Trevithick  went  to  Portugal  in 
1871  he  reported  that  the  Lisbon  Steam  Tramway  Co.

insisted  on  commencing  the  work.  The  company  soon 
went  into  liquidation,  and  a  number  of  lawsuits  under 
the  names  of  Twycross  v.  Grant  came  under  the  notice 
of  the  law  courts  in  1876-77-

ported to  be  of  the  length  of  a  taylor's  yard,  so  strong 
and  mighty  a  bow  the  Cornish  men  were  said  to  draw ! "

TUENER,  Thomas  (third  son  of  Edmund  Turner, 
d.  1821).  b.  Truro  16  Aug.  1796.  Educ.  at 
the  Truro  gram.  sch. ;  Apprentice  to  Nehe-

ology Manchester  Eoyal  Instit.  1843-73;

council  14  July  1865-July  1873,  being  the 
second  provincial  surgeon  so  elected;  One  of 
the  Founders  of  the  Salford  Sanitary  Associa- 
tion 1852.  d.  77  Mosley  st.  Manchester  17

On  provincial  medical  education,  an  essay  by 
T.  Turner  1817,  8". 

Outlines  of  a  system  of  medico-chirurgical 
education,  containing  illustrations  of  the  appli- 
cation of  anatomy,  physiology  and  other  sciences 
to  the  principal  practical  points  in  medicine  and 
surgery.  With  coloured  plates.  By  Thomas 
Turner... Lond.  printed  for  T.  and  G.  Under- 
wood; and  J.  Aston,  Manchester  [printed],  n.d. 
[1824],  8°.  Halftitle,  title,  dedication  and  errata 
4  leaves.  Introduction  pp.  i-viii.  The  text  pp. 
1-369,  Contents,  pp.  i-x,  10/6. 

A  practical  treatise  on  the  arterial  system. 
Intended  to  illustrate  the  importance  of  study- 
ing the  anastomoses,  in  reference  to  the  rationale 
of  the  new  operation  for  aneurism  ;  and  the 
surgical  treatment  of  hfemorrhage.  "With  origi- 
nal coloured  plans.  By  Thomas  Turner... Lond. 
printed  for  Longman ...  and  J.  Aston,  Manches- 
ter [printed]  1825,  8°.  Halftitle,  title  and  dedi- 
cation 3  leaves.  Contents,  introduction,  plan  and 
errata  pp.  i-xxv,  The  text  pp.  1-194,  8/-. 

An  address  to  the  inhabitants  of  Lancashire 
and  the  adjoining  counties  on  the  present  state 
of  the  medical  profession,  with  remarks  on  the 
elementary  education  of  the  student  and  the 
best  means  of  its  acquirement.  Intended  to  show 
the  practicability  and  importance  of  establishing 
a  school  (on  a  more  extended  scale)  in  Manches- 
ter, for  the  cultivation  of  medical  and  surgical 
knowledge.  By  Thomas  Turner.  Lond.  Long- 
man. . .  and  Eobinson  and  Bent,  Manchester  [print- 
ed] 1825,  8°.  pp.  vi,  26.

Manchester  Iris,  ii,  99-100  (1823). 

The  Philanthropist.  [On  causes  of  death]. 
Signed  S.   ib.  ii,  141.

general,   ib.  ii,  156-57. 

The  Philanthropist.  Essay  ii.  Mineral  poisons. 
On  acids,  [and]  On  alkalies,  ib.  ii,  181-82,  214. 
Introductory  address  to  the  students  at  the

street  Manchester  for  the  winter  session  of 
1840-41.  Provincial  Med.  and  Surg.  Journ.  i,  63 
(1840).

neighbourhood.
