UNWIN,  George.  Purser  of  one  of  the  H.E.I. 
Go's  ships  and  of  the  Royal  Navy;  Exported 
Tin  from  the  Malacca  isles  to  China  1787;  Ap- 
pointed Supervisor  of  the  exports  of  tin  from 
Cornwall  and  Devon  to  all  places  beyond  the 
Cape  of  Good  Hope  1791;  Resided  at  Stam- 
ford St.  London. 

Note. — The  H.E.I.  Co.  purchased  Cornish  tin  for 
export  from  1789  to  1817  when  their  connection  with 
Cornwall  ceased,  cf.  Hen.  Miglish's  Mining  Almanack 
1849,  p.  169. 

Letters,  remarks,  etc.  with  a  view  to  open  an 
extensive  trade  in  the  article  of  tin  from  the 
county  of  Cornwall  to  India,  Persia  and  China.

VANE,  Sir  Harry  (son  of  sir  Henry  Vane),  b. 
1612.  Governor  of  Massachusetts  1636-37; 
M.P.  Kingston  1640;  Imprisoned  in  a  castle 
in  the  isles  of  Scilly  1660  to  Mch.  1662.  Be- 
headed on  Tower  hill,  London  14  June  1662. 

Two  treatises  :  viz  :  I.  An  epistle  general  to 
the  mystical  body  of  Christ  on  earth,  the  church 
universal  in  Babylon ;  II.  The  face  of  the  times 
wherein  is  discovered,  the  rise,  progress  and 
issue,  of  the  enmity  and  contest,  between  the 
seed  of  the  woman  and  the  seed  of  the  serpent 
&c....Both  written  by  sir  Henry  Vane,  knight, 
in  the  time  of  his  imprisonment.  Printed  in  the 
year  1662,  12". 

Note.— General  title  1  leaf,  Title  to  An  epistle 
general  1  leaf,  To  the  Reader  3  leaves,  An  epistle  pp.  1- 
58,  Faces  of  the  times  pp.  59-93,  A  letter  of  sir  Henry 
Vane  to  his  lady  from  the  isle  of  Scilly,  dated  "  March 
7,  16f  i,"  pp.  94-99,  Errata  1  page. 

A  pilgrimage  into  the  land  of  promise  by  the 
light  of  the  vision  of  Jacob's  ladder  and  faith... 
Written  in  the  year  1662  by  Henry  Vane, 
knight,  towards  the  latter  end  of  his  prison 
state,  by  himself  fully  reviewed  and  perfected 
some  few  days  before  his  suffering... Printed  in 
the  yeare  1664,  4".  pp.  110.  The  head  lines 
read  "  A  treatise  concerning  eternal  life."

Note. — On  Tuesday  2  Jan.  169-|  a  quarrel  took  place 
at  a  tavern  in  Holborn  between  some  Cornish  gentlemen, 
Capt.  Trevanion  and  Mr.  Verman,  principals,  Capt. 
Carew  and  Mr.  Trevanion,  Mr.  Eawley  and  Mr.  Buller 
their  seconds,  they  went  in  the  night  into  Eed  Lyon 
fields  and  fought  when  Mr.  Verman  and  Mr.  Buller  were 
dangerously  wounded.

at  Penzance  ix,  176;  Her  majesty's  yacht  passing  the 
Longships  ix,  176;  Eoyal  visit  to  Place  house  ix,  177,

Fairy  yacht  in  Fal  river  near  Tregothnan  ix,  180;  Her 
majesty's  visit  to  Eestormel  castle  ix,  181 ;  Her  majesty's 
visit  to  the  isles  of  Scilly  xi,  120  (1847).

69.  m.  Miss  Fanny  de  Clanc6,  a  refugee  and

VINICOMBE,  Rev.  John  (son  of  John  Vini- 
combe  of  Madron,  m.  26,  Feb.  1759  a<  Madron, 
Susanna,  dau.  of  Will.  Boase.  She  was  bapt. 
Madron  12  May  1725).  Educ.  Penzance  gram, 
sch.,  Matric.  from  Pembroke  coll.  Oxf  3  Mch 
1778,  aged  18,  B.A.  12  Feb.  1782,  M.A.  24 
Oct.  1785,  B.D.  20  June  1799;  Senior  fellow 
of  his  coll. ;  Tutor  to  [Sir]  Rose  Price  at  Har- 
row and  at  Magd.  coll.  Oxf. ;  Made  a  tour  of 
Europe  with  him  and  accompanied  him  to 
Jamaica  where  he  remained  two  years;  Tutor 
of  Pemb.  coll.;  Public  examiner,  d.  29  Feb. 
1808  from  the  effects  of  a  fell  from  his  horse. 
cf.  Gent.  Mag.  Ixxvii,  275  (1808);  Account  of 
family  of  Boase  (1876)  col.  21.

VIVIAN,  Andrew.  Engineer,  Camborne ;  Ran 
a  locomotive  on  the  road  near  Truro  1801;  Con- 
ducted Trevithick's  locomotive  from  Leather

is  adopted."  With  2  diagrams.  Lond.  1873,  fol. 
pp.  6,  1/4.

12  Apl.  m.  at  Penryn,  a  dau.  of  Amos  Fish, 
shoemaker.  She  resides  (July  1877)  in  Bir- 
mingham, cf.  Congregational  Year  book  1875, 
pp.  371-72.

teeDth  chapter  of  the  Revelation  of  St.  John.

Cosmology.  An  enquiry  into  the  cause  of 
what  is  called  gravitation  or  attraction,  in  which 
the  motions  of  the  heavenly  bodies  and  the  pre- 
servation and  operation  of  all  nature  are  de- 
duced from  an  universal  principle  of  efflux  and 
reflux.  With  copper  plates.  By  T.  Vivian... 
Bath,  printed  and  sold  by  S.  Hazard,  sold  also

WALKEE,  Donald.

in  Cumberland,  Westmoreland,  Cornwall  and

WALKER,  Rev.  Samuel  Edmund  fo7ily  ehild 
of  Mr.  Walker,  barrister,  London),  b.  London. 
Of  Trinity  coll.  Camb.  B.A.  1831,  M.A.  1834, 
B.D.  1841;  E.  of  St.  Columb  Major  by  his 
father's  purchase  11  Mch.  1841  to  1869;  Re- 
stored the  rectory  house  at  an  expense  of 
£7000,  and  then  offered  the  living  as  in  part 
endowment  of  a  bishoprick  of  Cornwall ;  Em- 
ployed in  building  speculations  at  Gravesend 
and  Netting  hill,  London,  in  which  he  lost 
large  sums  of  money;  BuUt  the  ch.  of  St. 
Columba,  now  known  as  All  Saints,  Netting 
hill ;  Lived  abroad  for  some  years,  his  living 
being  sequestrated,  d  10  Eton  road,  Hamp- 
stead,  London  16  Mch.  1869,  aged  58.  bur. 
Friern-Barnet.  cf.  Beg.  and  Mag.  of  Biog.  i, 
401  (1869);  Cornioall  Ga.:ette  25  Mch.  1869,  p. 
8;  Western  M.  News  19  3Ich.  1869,  p.  2,  22

WAKBEN,  James.    (Con.). 

Note. — Whilst  on  his  voyage  to  Mexico  in  the 
"Cambria"  in  Feb.  1825,  the  ship  encountered  the 
"  Kent,"  East  Indiaman,  on  fire  in  the  Bay  of  Biscay. 
He  rendered  great  assistance  in  lifting  the  women  and 
children  in  their  passage  from  the  one  ship  to  the  other, 
but  in  so  doing  strained  himself;  from  the  effects  of  this 
he  never  perfectly  recovered.  He  was  allowed  an  annual 
pension  by  a  lady  whose  life  he  was  instrumental  in 
saving.

citor; Eesided  at  Truro,  d.  Park,  St,  Clement.

X,  98  (1860)  by  S.  C.  Freeman.  See  also  Davies  Gil- 
bert's Cornwall  iv,  91.

WESTON,  Egbert  Harcourt. 

Letters  and  important  documents  relative  to 
the  Edystone  lighthouse,  selected  chiefly  from 
the  correspondence  of  the  late  Eobert  Weston, 
esq.  and  from  other  manuscripts,  to  which  is 
added  a  report  made  to  the  lords  of  the  treasury 
in  1809  by  the  Trinity  corporation,  with  some 
observations  upon  that  report.  By  Eobert  Har- 
court Weston,  esq.  Embellished  with  two  views 
of  the  lighthouse,  one  representing  the  edifice 
as  it  appears  on  a  morning  after  a  storm  and 
the  other  in  a  calm.  Lond.  G.  and  W.  Nicol 
1811,  4».  pp.  XV,  308,  21/-. 

Note. — Eobert  Weston  was  at  one  time  proprietor 
-Of  the  Edystone  and  the  correspondence  above  referred 
to  is  in  reference  to  a  claim  made  by  him  on  the  Trinity 
house  for  a  portion  of  the  light  dues.

of  the  works  of  Albert  Durer  bequeathed  to  Whitaker 
by  NoUekens  and  "my  commented  copies  of  the  block- 
head Whitaker's  History  of  Manchester  and  his  Corn- 
wall Cathedral."  These  were  bequeathed  to  the  Br. 
Museum.  His  annotated  copy  of  the  Cathedral  of  Corn- 
wall is  now  marked  C,  28  1.  On  the  fly  leaf  is  written 
"  It  is  my  desire  that  after  my  decease,  this  volume,  as 
well  as  my  quarto  edition  of  Whitaker's  History  of 
Manchester  in  two  volumes,  be  presented  to  the  British 
Museum."  The  annotations  are  of  the  most  caustic 
nature,  the  title  page  of  vol.  ii  is  altered  to  "The 
second  part  of  an  antiqvarian  hash  or  salmagvndi  under 
the  whimsical  appellation  of  the  Ancient  cathedral  of 
Cornwall  pantagrvelically  surveyed  by  John  Whitaker 
B.D.  Hector  of  Buan-Lanyhorne."  At  the  conclusion 
is  found  this  sentence  "I  should  not  have  taken  the 
trouble  of  criticising  this  tiresome  and  chostic  work, 
if  the  author  had  not  unwarrantably  attacked  and  to- 
tally misconceived  [viz.  in  ii,  'A(,5  ' seq.  of  the  above 
work]  the  design  of  my  essay  in  the  Archmologia  on  the 
names  of  the  pieces  in  the  game  of  chess  in  a  manner 
which  at  once  displays  excessive  arrogance  and  most 
profound  ignorance." 

The  life  of  St.  Neot  the  oldest  of  all  the  broth- 
ers to  king  Alfred.  By  the  rev.  John  Whitaker 
...Lond.  John  Joseph  Stockdale  1809,  8°.  pp 
viii,  381,  10/6. 

Note.-— The  preface  is  signed  "J.  J.  Srtockdalel 
Pall  Mall,  Dec.  28,  1808."  Mr.  Whitaker  having  died 
30  Oct.  1808  after  correcting  the  proofs  as  far  as  p.  208.

Wildman  who  was  also  the  chief  proprietor.  Eleven

sale  The  series  of  abstracts  called  "  Sixty-five  years 
ago"  was  contributed  by  A.  C.  Wildman.  A  portion  of 
this  journal  was  printed  in  London  by  Messrs.  Cassell 
as  an  illustrated  monthly  journal  for  various  customers.

Gazette  under  the  signature  of  "Whachum,"  to  The

WILLIAMS,  William.    (Con.). 

Note. — On  the  page  facing  the  title  are  the  follow- 
ing verses : 

An  Advertisement. 
An  author  of  my  name,  hath  lately  writ 
A  sacred  book  in  verse,  and  some  thought  it 
To  be  my  study ;  and  their  reason  why, 
Because  mine  poems  were,  and  piety. 
But  to  content  the  world,  I  dare  it  tell 
This  author's  writings  mine  may  much  excel  : 
Moreover,  thus  I  differ  from  that  man, 
He's  Cornwall  bom,  and  I  am  Cardigan: 
And  likewise  in  our  age,  we  disagree, 
I  am  near  thirty;  he  near  sixty-three.

events  MS. 

Note. — This  MS.  belonging  to  the  Penzance  Nat. 
Hist,  and  Antiq.  Soc.  commences  with  an  account  ex- 
tracted from  Carew  of  the  landing  of  the  Spaniards  in 
Cornwall  in  1595,  next  mentions  the  breaking  down  of 
Newlyn  Pier  by  a  heavy  storm  on  25  Nov.  1738,  and  then 
proceeds  as  a  regular  journal  with  the  year  1771,  it  has 
continuous  entries  from  that  date  to  1836.

and  Hertford  st.  Mayfair,  London;  Purchased 
Eushton  hall  estate,  Northampton  in  1828 
for  £140,000  sold  it  in  Sep.  1854  for  £165,000; 
High  Sheriff  of  Northamptonshire  1832;  Re- 
sided in  Paris  for  many  years  before  his  de- 
cease, d.  found  dead  in  his  bed  at  his  hotel  131 
Eue  St.  Dominique,  Faubourg  St.  Germain, 
Paris  21  Jan.  1855  being  the  last  male  heir  of 
the  family,  cf  Gent.  Mag.  xliv,  652-54  (1855); 
Annual  Register  1855,  Chron.  p.  244;  Tlu  Atlas 
(London  Newspaper)  17  Mch.  1855,  p.  175,  21

The  spiritual  register  or  an  authentic  account 
of  the  Lord'.s  dealings  with  many  believers  who 
have  lately  departed  in  the  triumph  of  faith.  To

of  God  on  the  impenitent  and  ungodly.  By 
Thomas  Wills.  Lond.  Hughes  and  Walsh  1784- 
87,  2  vols.  12°.  7/6,  Vol.  3,  1795,  12".  2/6.

Christ,  a  Christian's  Life :  or  a  practical  dis- 
course of  a  believer's  life  derived  from  Christ 
and  resolved  into  Christ,  being  the  substance  of 
several  sermons,  preached  by  the  Author  upon 
his  recovery  of  a  fit  of  sickness  and  since  ex- 
torted from  him  by  the  importunity  of  his

WILLYAMS,  Sabah.     (Con.J. 

Ixxxii,  pt.  i,  21-24  (1812),  xv,  810  (1863); 
niust.  L.  News  xliii,  655  (1863);  Jewish  Chron- 
icle 18  Dec.  1863,  p.  5. 

Note. — The  will  was  proved  in  the  London  court 
on  U  Deo.  1863  by  the  rt.  hon.  B.  Disraeli  the  sarviv- 
ing  executor,  the  personalty  being  sworn  under  £40,000. 
Mrs.  Willyams  after  leaving  legacies  to  the  amount  of 
£6000  gave  the  remainder  of  her  property  to  Mr.  Dis- 
raeli in  these  words:  "  In  testimony  of  my  affection 
and  of  my  approbation  and  admiration  of  his  efforts  to 
vindicate  the  race  of  Israel,  with  my  views  he  is  ac- 
quainted, and  will  no  doubt  endeavour  to  accomplish 
them."  She  further  expi essed  her  wish  that  he  would 
obtain  the  queen's  permission  to  use  the  surname  and 
arms  of  the  family  of  Lara  and  Mendez  de  Costa  in  ad- 
dition to  or  precedent  to  that  of  Disraeli.  Mr.  Disraeli 
is  said  to  have  become  acquainted  with  Mrs.  Willyams 
by  answering  her  enquiry  as  to  whether  he  was  descend- 
ed from  a  Spanish  family.

The  king's  servant  T.  Winter,   archdeacon  of

liam Bodye,  his  archdeaconry  for  3  years  to  3

penes  Lcrrd  HatJterton  at  Teddesley,  Stafford.

bitter  spar  and  iron  spar.  Philos.  Mag.  xxxix, 
455-58  (1812);  Philos.  Trans,  cii,  Pt.  i,  159-62 
(1812). 

Note. — The  iron  spar  came  from  Maudlin  mine 
near  Lostwithiel.

the  demise  of  Hanley  against  Wood  18  June

Aug.  1845.  Came  to  reside  at  Penzance  1850;

der palace,  Sep.  1877.  Unm.  cf.  Musical  World

Devonia,  a  poem  in  five  cantos,  descriptive  of

A  view  of  the  present  state  of  the  Scilly  isles, 
exhibiting  their  vast  importance  to  the  British 
empire;  the  improvements  of  which  they  are  sus- 
ceptible and  a  particular  account  of  the  means 
lately  adopted  for  the  amelioration  of  the  con- 
dition of  the  inhabitants,  by  the  establishment 
and  extension  of  their  fisheries.  By  the  rev.  G. 
Woodley ...  Embellished  with  accurate  charts. 
Lond.  F.  C.  and  J.  Rivington;  [J.]  Carthew

large  folio  of  4  pages  at  2d.  On  the  appearance  of  the 
4  Oct.  1855  issue  the  title  given  was  "The  western  news, 
Bath  and  Taunton  reporter."  On  the  29  Oct.  1855  it 
was  "  The  western  news  and  penny  courier. "  This  paper 
is  now  called  "The  western  news"  pp.  8,  Id.

WTLLIE. 


913 


WYNNE. 


WYATT,  William.    (Coii.J. 

Mr.  Justice  Chambre  on  a  Thursday,  but  by  mistake  it 
was  supposed  to  be  Friday  and  in  passing  sentence,  the 
execution  was  directed  to  be  on  the  Monday  instead  of 
the  Saturday.  The  next  morning  on  the  mistake  being 
discovered  the  execution  vms  ordered  for  the  Saturday. 
An  order  was  then  made  pursuant  to  the  authority  given 
by  25  Oeo.  ii  c.  37  to  stay  the  execution  in  order  to  take 
the  opinion  of  the  judges  upon  the  case.  In  Easter  term 
2.5  Apl.  1812  this  case  was  taken  into  consideration  at  a 
meeting  of  all  the  judges,  except  Mansfield,  C.J.  and 
Lawrence  J,  when  they  agreed  that  an  error  in  pronounc- 
ing the  sentence  might  be  set  right  during  the  assizes. 
cf.  Russell  and  Ryan's  Crown  Cases  (1825)  pp.  230-32.

Mr.  Hunter  Bodwell,  Q.C.,  Mr.  E.  Harris  and  Mr.  G.  C. 
Alexander  were  for  the  petitioner,  and  Serjeant  Ballan- 
tine  and  Mr.  Bowen  were  for  the  sitting  member.  Judg- 
ment was  delivered  on  24  Feb.  to  this  effect  "  that  the

Note. — Thos.  James  was  b.  near  Mithian  16  Oct. 
1784,  a  miner,  converted  to  Wesleyanism  1807,  class 
leader  and  superintendent  of  the  Sunday  sch.  d.  Mithian 
27  Mch.  1853.

spirit  merchant  and  shipping  agent,  Falmouth.
